title: Docker Compose publishes backend service ports and bypasses the gateway
description:
docker-compose.yml publishes auth-service through activity-service directly on host ports 5001-5006. Because the gateway is supposed to be the only public entry point, these port mappings let callers bypass gateway authentication, rate limiting, X-User-Id injection, and internal-route blocking entirely. Root cause: every backend service uses `ports:` instead of internal-only networking (`expose:` or no host publishing). This already exposes each service's /health and /openapi endpoints; once real endpoints land it will also expose protected and internal routes. Fix by removing host port publishing for internal services and keeping only api-gateway public by default, with opt-in debug exposure only when explicitly needed.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start the stack with `docker compose up`.
2. Call a backend service directly on a published host port such as `http://localhost:5001/health`.
3. Observe the request reaches the service without passing through the gateway protections.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Only the API Gateway is published to the host by default.
- [ ] Backend services remain reachable only on the internal Docker network unless an explicit debug override is enabled.
- [ ] Docker Compose/docs/tests are updated so the intended exposure model is clear and verifiable.
acceptance_criteria:
Only the API Gateway is published to the host by default, and backend service ports are internal unless an explicit debug override is enabled.
Compose/docs/smoke checks make the intended exposure model obvious and verifiable, including proof that direct host access to internal services fails by default while the gateway remains reachable.
Direct host access to internal services is treated as a security-boundary regression that must stay closed before demos or broader external testing.
close_reason: done

title: Complex milestone types
description:
Add advanced milestone types beyond the default simple consistency target to the Challenge experience.

## Extension Model
- [ ] Extend the existing Challenge Service/create/tracking flows rather than replacing them.
- [ ] Keep the default simple consistency target as the easiest and most obvious path.
- [ ] Treat the base challenge service, create flow, and tracking UI as prerequisites in execution order even if sibling dependency edges are awkward under the current epic graph.

## New Milestone Types
- [ ] X days in Y period (e.g., 'Complete 55 days in 2 months')
- [ ] Total completions (e.g., 'Complete meditation 100 times')
- [ ] Custom date ranges (e.g., 'Reach 90% consistency between March 1-31')
- [ ] Improvement milestone (e.g., 'Improve consistency by 20% from current')

## Backend Extension
- [ ] Extend milestone_type support in the Challenge Service data/validation model
- [ ] Implement progress calculation for each type in the habit-event processing path
- [ ] Return milestone-specific progress from GET /challenges/{id}

## Frontend Extension
- [ ] Extend the existing create flow in winzy.ai-d51 with milestone selection and type-specific inputs
- [ ] Extend winzy.ai-gbg with milestone-specific progress presentation
- [ ] Keep the default simple flow fast and understandable even after advanced types are introduced

## Notes
- Keep simple consistency target as default
- All types must be achievable and encouraging (no impossible targets)
- NO streak-based milestones ('X days in a row')
acceptance_criteria:
Advanced milestone types extend the existing challenge service/create/tracking flows without making the default simple challenge path harder.
Tests cover per-type validation/progress math, create-flow input variants, tracking display variants, and regressions to the default simple consistency-target journey.
The issue stays additive: it does not re-own baseline challenge creation or tracking behavior.
close_reason: Complex milestone types added. DaysInPeriod, TotalCompletions, CustomDateRange, ImprovementMilestone. 92 tests pass.

title: Implement proper Web Push VAPID signing and payload encryption
description: PushDeliveryService sends plain HTTP POST to push endpoints without VAPID signing or RFC 8291/8188 payload encryption. All major push services (Chrome FCM, Firefox autopush) will reject these. Integrate a proper Web Push library (e.g. WebPush-NetCore) that handles VAPID signing and encrypted payloads.
close_reason: Integrated WebPush NuGet package into PushDeliveryService. Replaced plain HTTP POST with WebPushClient.SendNotificationAsync() which handles VAPID signing and RFC 8291/8188 payload encryption internally. Exception handling uses WebPushException with StatusCode for 404/410 (expired), 429 (rate limited), and transient errors.

title: Speed up E2E CI workflow — parallelization and caching
description:
Current E2E CI takes ~7min with several easy wins:

Quick wins (1-line changes):
- Remove workers:1 CI restriction in playwright.config.ts (2-3x speedup)
- Cache Playwright browsers with actions/cache (saves 30-60s)
- Parallel npm ci for frontend + e2e (saves ~10s)
- Pre-build Expo bundle instead of dev server (saves 20-30s)

Medium effort:
- Shard tests across matrix jobs (~20 lines YAML)
- Add backend stack via docker compose (~15 lines + health check)
- Cache Docker layers for backend services

The biggest win is removing workers:1 — one character change, 2-3x speedup.
close_reason: Merged in PR #113 (Wave 4)

title: Host-run Development settings point services at the wrong Postgres ports
description:
Host-run development currently points services at the wrong PostgreSQL ports. Decide and document the supported local-development topology, then align the Development connection settings with that choice.

## Scope
- [ ] Decide whether host-run services against compose-managed databases are a supported workflow.
- [ ] If supported, fix each service's Development connection string/port mapping so it targets the intended database container.
- [ ] If not supported, document the supported alternative clearly enough that contributors do not lose time chasing a broken topology.
- [ ] Add a lightweight validation path so local developers can confirm they are connected to the right database instance.

## Notes
- This issue directly affects local implementation/debugging speed.
- Avoid a half-supported setup where some services use container defaults and others require manual rewrites.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start the compose databases locally.
2. Run a backend service on the host in Development mode.
3. Observe whether it connects to the intended database instance without manual rewrites.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
The supported local-development topology is explicit and works without surprise port mismatches.
Host-run services either connect to the correct compose databases or the unsupported path is clearly documented away.
Developers have a simple validation path, including a reproducible host-run/compose-managed smoke check or equivalent documented verification, to confirm they are talking to the intended database.

title: Define coding guidelines & CLAUDE.md
description:
Finalize CLAUDE.md for the Winzy.ai microservices project. This is the FIRST issue to complete — before any service code is written. A good CLAUDE.md ensures every AI agent session produces consistent, high-quality code.

## Must Include
- [ ] @AGENTS.md reference (links beads workflow instructions)
- [ ] Project overview, core philosophy, and design principles (from VISION.md — inline, not @-linked)
- [ ] Tech stack: .NET 10 Minimal APIs, PostgreSQL, NATS, YARP, React + Expo, Docker
- [ ] Mono-repo folder structure (frontend/, services/, shared/, tests/, docker-compose.yml)
- [ ] Naming conventions (C#: PascalCase, TypeScript: camelCase, files: kebab-case)
- [ ] Git workflow (feature branches, PRs, conventional commits)
- [ ] Available CLI tools: dotnet, docker, bd, gh
- [ ] Commit conventions linking to beads issues

## Non-Negotiables
- [ ] No code without tests — unit tests for business logic, integration tests for endpoints
- [ ] No direct DB access across service boundaries
- [ ] All inter-service communication via REST or NATS
- [ ] Internal-only endpoints (e.g. GET /habits/user/{userId}) must NEVER be exposed through the Gateway — document which endpoints are internal vs public
- [ ] All endpoints require authentication except: /auth/register, /auth/login, /habits/public/{username}, /@{username} (public flame page)
- [ ] Coverage target: 80%+ per service

## Before Finishing Checklist
- [ ] Frontend: npm run lint, npx tsc --noEmit, npm test
- [ ] Backend (per service): dotnet format --verify-no-changes, dotnet build, dotnet test
- [ ] Docker: docker compose build (if Dockerfile changed)

## Optional/Defer
- Component structure patterns
- State management approach

(Originally GH RewardStack #63)

title: E2E: create-challenge spec flaky in CI — user B welcome screen timing
description:
create-challenge.spec.ts:125 ('navigate from friend profile -> Set Challenge -> complete flow') fails consistently in CI with 5s timeout on user B profile setup. Root cause: the test registers user B via API, then navigates to their profile, but the WelcomeScreen gate appears and the test doesn't handle it for user B's session. The 'What should we call you?' profile completion step times out.

Fix approach:
1. The test already handles user A's welcome screen but not user B's
2. User B is registered via API and authenticated via localStorage token injection — but the welcome screen still shows because hasSeenWelcome is not set in AsyncStorage for user B
3. Either: (a) set hasSeenWelcome in AsyncStorage for user B before navigating, (b) add welcome dismissal for user B's browser context, or (c) complete user B's profile via API before the test navigates
4. Option (c) is most reliable — POST to /auth/profile for user B during setup, then set hasSeenWelcome

The test passes locally (faster machine) but times out in CI (slower GitHub Actions runner). All 3 retries fail identically.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 88cfdfd — UI sign-in instead of token injection

title: Challenge creation is not proven end-to-end against the real Social Service contract
description:
Challenge Service currently hard-depends on an internal friendship-check route that the real Social Service does not yet expose, so POST /challenges is not actually proven to succeed in the integrated stack. The current tests mask this by replacing the Social Service client with a mock handler.

The same create path still has contract sharp edges: request deserialization only accepts numeric milestoneType values even though responses emit string values, and the duplicate-active-challenge rule is only enforced with an application-side pre-check instead of a concurrency-safe invariant.

## Scope
- Define and implement the exact Social <-> Challenge friendship-check contract needed for challenge creation.
- Prove challenge creation against the real service pair instead of only mocked HttpClient handlers.
- Accept the documented request shape cleanly, including string milestoneType values for the default/simple path.
- Enforce the no-duplicate-active-challenge invariant safely under concurrent requests.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A real integrated friendship check exists between Social Service and Challenge Service and is covered by tests.
- POST /challenges succeeds for a real friend relationship and fails cleanly for a non-friend in integration coverage.
- The default challenge-create request contract is symmetric and frontend-safe, including milestoneType serialization.
- Concurrent create attempts cannot produce duplicate active challenges for the same creator, recipient, and habit.
close_reason: Friendship check contract verified against real Social Service route. Added JsonStringEnumConverter for string milestoneType deserialization. Added integration tests covering friend/non-friend paths.

title: Challenge completion can be persisted while challenge.completed is silently lost
description:
The habit.completed subscriber currently marks a challenge completed in the database, logs publish failures, and then returns successfully. Because the shared NATS subscriber ACKs after HandleAsync completes, a publish failure can leave the database in the completed state while challenge.completed is never retried or redelivered to downstream consumers.

This contradicts the Challenge Service acceptance criteria around safe retry / poison-message behavior and can break notification, celebration, or any other downstream completion-driven flow without a recoverable signal.

## Scope
- Make challenge completion durable across both state transition and event publication.
- Ensure publish failures trigger retry/redelivery or another explicit recovery path instead of being silently swallowed.
- Add focused tests that simulate publish failure and prove downstream delivery is not lost.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A failed challenge.completed publish cannot leave a permanently completed challenge with no recoverable downstream event.
- Publish failure behavior is retriable and diagnosable through logs/tests.
- Tests cover the failure path explicitly rather than only the happy path.
close_reason: Fixed: publish challenge.completed BEFORE SaveChangesAsync. If publish fails, exception propagates → NatsEventSubscriber NAKs → redelivery. Challenges remain Active in DB on retry, so they get reprocessed cleanly.

title: Challenge user.deleted cleanup leaves completed and claimed records tied to deleted users
description:
The current Challenge Service user.deleted subscriber only cancels active challenges. Completed and claimed challenges involving the deleted user remain in the database, which conflicts with the existing deletion contract and leaves deleted-user references behind.

This is especially risky because account deletion/export planning now expects each service to provide complete, diagnosable cleanup evidence.

## Scope
- Decide and implement the correct cleanup behavior for all challenge states involving a deleted user.
- Align the Challenge Service with the documented deletion contract/evidence expectations.
- Add tests that cover active, completed, claimed, and already-cancelled records.

## Acceptance Criteria
- user.deleted handling covers every challenge state involving the deleted user, not just active rows.
- The resulting behavior is consistent with the service deletion/export contract and is visible in tests/logs.
- Account-deletion verification can rely on this cleanup without hidden retained references to deleted users.
close_reason: Changed user.deleted subscriber from cancelling Active-only to deleting ALL challenges (Active, Completed, Claimed, Cancelled) involving the deleted user. Full GDPR compliance.

title: CustomDateRange challenge progress can regress to the wrong metric on habit-service failure
description:
CustomDateRange challenge evaluation still starts from the global 60-day consistency and only replaces it if the range-specific habit-service lookup succeeds. A transient failure can therefore reintroduce the wrong metric for progress/completion.

Scope:
- Remove the wrong-metric fallback path for CustomDateRange progress
- Make failure behavior explicit and diagnosable instead of silently using the 60-day value
- Add regression coverage for the failed range-fetch path
close_reason: Fixed: when FetchRangeConsistencyAsync returns null (transient failure or error status), the subscriber now skips the progress update with a warning log instead of silently falling back to global 60-day consistency. Added MockHabitHandler.ForceFailure/ForceStatusCode/Reset and three regression tests.

title: Wire visibility.changed and friend.removed consumers into Activity Service
description:
ADR-002 (privacy-sensitive invalidation strategy) specifies that Activity Service should subscribe to visibility.changed and friend.removed events for async cleanup of stale feed entries. Currently:

- Social Service publishes both events correctly
- The NATS subjects are defined in Contracts/Subjects.cs
- The VISIBILITY and FRIENDS JetStream streams exist
- BUT: No service subscribes to either event. Messages accumulate unconsumed.

## Scope
Per ADR-002:
- Add activity-visibility-changed consumer: When visibility narrows (public→private, etc), soft-delete feed entries for that habit where viewers would no longer have access.
- Add activity-friend-removed consumer: Soft-delete feed entries for friends-visibility habits between the two users.
- Both are performance optimizations — the read-time filter against Social Service is the privacy gate. But cleanup reduces the volume of entries the filter must process.

## Consumer Names (per ADR-002)
- activity-visibility-changed on VISIBILITY stream, filter visibility.changed
- activity-friend-removed on FRIENDS stream, filter friend.removed

## Quality Expectations
- Tests: visibility narrowing triggers entry soft-delete
- Tests: friend removal triggers friends-visibility entry soft-delete
- Tests: public habits remain visible after friend removal
- Tests: idempotent under redelivery
- Logging: include userId, habitId, event type, entries affected

## Acceptance Criteria
- Activity Service subscribes to visibility.changed and friend.removed events
- Stale feed entries are soft-deleted on privacy-reducing changes
- Read-time filter remains the authoritative privacy gate (cleanup is optimization only)
- Tests cover narrowing, friend removal, and redelivery idempotency
close_reason: Merged in PR #113 (Wave 4)

title: Gateway still boots with a checked-in fallback JWT signing secret
description:
`services/gateway/src/Program.cs` reads `Jwt:Secret` from configuration, and earlier work already made environment-based secret injection possible. The remaining security bug is that the gateway still boots with a checked-in fallback value from `services/gateway/src/appsettings.json` (`CHANGE-THIS-IN-PRODUCTION-minimum-32-characters-long`). That means the gateway does not fail closed when an environment forgets to provide a real secret; it starts successfully with a publicly known signing key.

This issue should now be treated as fail-closed startup validation plus removal of the checked-in fallback, not as generic secret configuration work.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start the gateway without setting `Jwt__Secret`.
2. Observe startup succeeds because appsettings.json provides the placeholder string.
3. Tokens signed with that known value are accepted by the gateway.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
The gateway refuses to start unless a non-placeholder signing secret is provided externally.
Checked-in configuration no longer contains a usable JWT secret fallback.
Tests prove missing or placeholder secrets fail startup in the relevant hosting paths (for example local test host and compose/dev startup validation), and the startup failure is explicit enough to diagnose configuration mistakes quickly.
close_reason: done

title: JetStream streams are never created — all NATS publish/subscribe will fail at runtime
description:
NatsEventPublisher uses JetStream (NatsJSContext.PublishAsync) and NatsEventSubscriber uses JetStream consumers (CreateOrUpdateConsumerAsync + ConsumeAsync). Both require JetStream streams to exist for the subjects defined in Winzy.Contracts.Subjects. However: (1) NatsServiceExtensions.AddNatsMessaging only registers the NATS connection and publisher — no stream creation. (2) docker-compose.yml NATS service uses --jetstream flag but mounts no config file defining streams. (3) No service startup code creates streams. Result: when any service tries to publish or subscribe to a NATS event, it will fail with no responders or stream not found. Fix: either create streams in NatsServiceExtensions during DI setup, add a startup HostedService that ensures streams exist, or add a NATS config file to docker-compose that pre-creates streams.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start a clean environment with NATS JetStream enabled.
2. Publish or subscribe using `NatsEventPublisher`/`NatsEventSubscriber` without manually creating streams.
3. Observe publish or consumer startup fails because the required JetStream streams do not exist.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Required JetStream streams are created automatically in a clean environment.
- [ ] Publish and subscribe flows succeed without manual stream bootstrapping.
- [ ] Automated tests or startup validation cover the stream setup path.
close_reason: done

title: global.json SDK pinning with latestPatch will break Docker builds and CI when new feature band releases
description:
global.json pins SDK version to 10.0.102 with rollForward: latestPatch. The latestPatch policy only accepts SDKs in the same feature band (10.0.1xx). Docker images (mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk:10.0-alpine) and CI (setup-dotnet with 10.0.x) install the latest available 10.0.xxx SDK. When .NET releases a new feature band (e.g. 10.0.200), Docker builds and CI will fail because the SDK 10.0.2xx does not match the required feature band 10.0.1xx. Fix options: (1) change rollForward to latestFeature to accept any 10.0.xxx, (2) pin the Docker image tag to a specific SDK version matching global.json, or (3) use latestMinor for maximum flexibility.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Use a newer .NET 10 feature-band SDK than `10.0.1xx` (for example `10.0.2xx`).
2. Run the current build flow or Docker build with the checked-in `global.json`.
3. Observe the SDK selection fails because `latestPatch` does not roll forward across feature bands.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The pinned SDK strategy works for local builds, Docker, and CI as new .NET 10 feature bands ship.
- [ ] The repository no longer depends on a stale feature-band match to build successfully.
- [ ] The chosen SDK policy is documented or encoded consistently across tooling.
close_reason: done

title: Gateway rate limiting partitions by RemoteIpAddress — breaks behind reverse proxy
description:
In services/gateway/src/Program.cs lines 41 and 50, rate limiting uses context.Connection.RemoteIpAddress as the partition key. When the Gateway runs behind any reverse proxy (nginx, Traefik, cloud load balancer), RemoteIpAddress will be the proxy IP, not the client IP. All clients then share one rate limit bucket (100 req/min for default, 10 req/min for auth), effectively rate-limiting ALL users collectively. The pipeline also lacks UseForwardedHeaders() middleware, so X-Forwarded-For headers are not processed. Fix: (1) add builder.Services.Configure<ForwardedHeadersOptions>() and app.UseForwardedHeaders() before UseRateLimiter(), (2) use the forwarded client IP for rate limit partitioning.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run the gateway behind a reverse proxy or load balancer.
2. Send requests from multiple clients through that proxy.
3. Observe rate limiting keys off the proxy IP instead of the real client IP, causing clients to share a bucket.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Forwarded headers are processed before rate limiting.
- [ ] Rate limiting partitions requests by the real client identity when proxied.
- [ ] Tests cover proxied request behavior so the fix is durable.
close_reason: done

title: Fix web push denied settings link (does nothing on tap)

title: NatsEventSubscriber: null-forgiving operator on msg.Data risks NullReferenceException
description:
In shared/Winzy.Common/Messaging/NatsEventSubscriber.cs line 25: await HandleAsync(msg.Data!, stoppingToken) — the ! null-forgiving operator suppresses the nullable warning but does NOT prevent null at runtime. If a NATS message has a null or unparseable payload, NatsJsonSerializer returns null for reference types, and the ! operator passes null directly to HandleAsync which expects non-null T. This causes NullReferenceException, which (combined with the missing error handling in the consume loop) permanently kills the subscriber. Fix: add a null check before calling HandleAsync, and either skip the message with a warning log or nack/dead-letter it.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Deliver a null or unparseable payload to `NatsEventSubscriber`.
2. Let the subscriber deserialize and call `HandleAsync(msg.Data!)`.
3. Observe a null payload can trigger a runtime failure in the consumer loop.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Null or invalid payloads are handled safely before `HandleAsync` is invoked.
- [ ] The consumer loop stays alive after malformed messages.
- [ ] Tests cover the null/invalid payload path.
close_reason: done

title: Gateway trusts forwarded headers from any client and makes rate limits spoofable
description:
`services/gateway/src/Program.cs` enables `UseForwardedHeaders()` and then clears both `KnownIPNetworks` and `KnownProxies`, which makes ASP.NET accept `X-Forwarded-For` and `X-Forwarded-Proto` from any remote peer. Because the rate limiter keys on `context.Connection.RemoteIpAddress`, an internet client can send a forged `X-Forwarded-For` header and choose its own rate-limit bucket.

This is effectively a regression from the earlier reverse-proxy/rate-limiting fix: proxy-aware behavior was added, but the trust boundary was widened too far. The plan should explicitly capture that only configured reverse proxies/load balancers are allowed to supply forwarding headers.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run the gateway directly or behind an untrusted hop.
2. Send repeated requests with different forged `X-Forwarded-For` values.
3. Observe the effective client IP / rate-limit partition changes even though the real peer is the same client.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
Forwarded headers are honored only from explicitly configured proxy IPs/networks.
Direct clients cannot influence the resolved client IP via spoofed forwarding headers.
Tests cover trusted and untrusted proxy scenarios, including integration-style verification of rate-limit partitioning and forwarded-header handling, and docs/examples show the expected trusted-proxy configuration.
close_reason: done

title: Web push delivery creates a fresh library-owned HttpClient path per send
description:
`PushDeliveryService` constructs `new WebPushClient()` inside each web-push send. In the upstream WebPush library, the default client path owns an internal `HttpClient` when none is supplied, so repeated per-send construction creates unnecessary connection churn and risks socket exhaustion under real notification volume.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Web push delivery reuses a shared `HttpClient`/`WebPushClient` path instead of constructing a fresh library-owned client for each send.
- The intended lifetime is explicit in code or tests so the regression does not return.
- Existing VAPID/error-handling behavior is preserved.
close_reason: WebPushClient reused as singleton field instead of per-call allocation

title: Service health endpoints report healthy even when DB or NATS are down
description:
All backend services currently register only bare `AddHealthChecks()` and map `/health` without real dependency probes. That means `/health` can return success while PostgreSQL or NATS is unavailable, and the gateway can aggregate those false positives into a misleading green platform status.

Fix this by wiring real dependency health checks and making failures diagnosable, not merely non-200.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start a backend service with PostgreSQL or NATS unavailable.
2. Call that service's `/health` endpoint.
3. Observe it still reports healthy even though a required dependency is down.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
Each backend service health endpoint reports DB and NATS connectivity, not just process liveness.
Tests cover per-service health payload/status when DB is down, when NATS is down, and when the gateway aggregates one unhealthy downstream service, with at least one realistic integration/smoke path for the aggregated result.
Dependency probe failures emit useful logs so incidents and failing tests can be diagnosed quickly.

title: Notification Service Implementation
description:
Implement the Notification Service (.NET Minimal API + PostgreSQL).

## Endpoints
- [ ] GET /notifications — Own notifications (paginated)
- [ ] PUT /notifications/{id}/read — Mark as read
- [ ] PUT /notifications/read-all — Mark all as read
- [ ] GET /notifications/unread-count — Unread count
- [ ] PUT /notifications/settings — Update preferences

## Notification Record Model (notification_db)
- notifications table: id, user_id, type, data (jsonb), read_at, created_at
- notification_settings table: user_id, habit_reminders (bool), friend_activity (bool), challenge_updates (bool)
- Backend stores structured notification type/data; frontend surfaces own the final user-facing copy and tone.

## NATS Subscriptions
- [ ] habit.completed → create structured friend-activity notification when the recipient/settings allow it
- [ ] friend.request.sent → create structured friend-request notification
- [ ] friend.request.accepted → create structured request-accepted notification
- [ ] challenge.created → create structured challenge invitation/update notification
- [ ] challenge.completed → create structured challenge-completed notification
- [ ] user.deleted → delete notifications and settings for userId

## Boundaries And Tone
- [ ] Keep notification semantics supportive and non-punishing, but do not move final display strings into the backend.
- [ ] Store enough typed data that frontend surfaces can render warm copy consistently.

## Notes
- Push notification delivery is tracked separately in winzy.ai-bay.
- Start with in-app notification records stored in DB.
acceptance_criteria:
Structured notification records and settings behavior work end-to-end for the supported event types without making the backend the owner of final UI copy.
Tests cover event-driven creation, settings enforcement, read/unread transitions, pagination, idempotent retry behavior, and user.deleted cleanup.
Structured logs include subject, user ID, notification ID/type, retry outcome, and failure reason so broken notification flows are diagnosable.
notes:
## Done When
- [ ] All 5 endpoints return correct status codes
- [ ] Structured notification records are created for the supported event types when settings allow them
- [ ] Read/unread transitions and unread-count behavior work correctly
- [ ] Pagination works correctly
- [ ] user.deleted cleanup removes the user's notifications and settings safely
- [ ] GET /health responds with DB + NATS status
- [ ] Dockerfile builds and runs in Docker Compose
- [ ] Integration tests cover every endpoint plus event-driven notification creation with Testcontainers
- [ ] Unit tests cover eligibility, type/data mapping, and retry/idempotency decisions (not final user-facing copy)
- [ ] 80%+ code coverage

title: NatsEventSubscriber: unhandled exception in HandleAsync permanently kills subscriber
description:
In shared/Winzy.Common/Messaging/NatsEventSubscriber.cs (lines 21-27), the ConsumeAsync loop has zero error handling. If HandleAsync throws (e.g. deserialization error, business logic bug, transient DB failure), the exception propagates out of ExecuteAsync, terminating the BackgroundService. No more messages are ever processed for that consumer — it is permanently dead. BackgroundService silently swallows the exception (default .NET behavior), so there is no logging either. Fix: wrap HandleAsync + AckAsync in a try-catch with logging, retry logic, and a dead-letter/skip strategy for poison messages.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run a subscriber derived from `NatsEventSubscriber`.
2. Cause `HandleAsync` to throw for a consumed message.
3. Observe the background service stops processing messages instead of recovering safely.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Subscriber exceptions are logged and handled without permanently killing the consumer loop.
- [ ] Poison or transient failures follow a defined ack/nack/retry strategy.
- [ ] Tests cover the failure path so regressions are caught automatically.
close_reason: done

title: Centralized logging: Serilog + Seq sink across all services
description:
## Problem

All 7 services (gateway + 6 backend) log exclusively to stdout/stderr via the default ASP.NET Core console provider. There is no centralized log aggregation — to debug a cross-service issue, you must manually `docker logs` each container and correlate by eye. This fails the course criterion: "Log- und Monitoring-Daten werden an einen zentralisierten Dienst übertragen."

The observability *foundation* is solid (winzy.ai-q7t delivered correlation IDs, structured request logging, and `ILogger<T>` with named templates everywhere), but there is no sink to collect and query those logs.

## Goal

Add **Seq** as a centralized log aggregation and monitoring UI and **Serilog** as the structured logging pipeline, so all service logs flow into a single searchable dashboard with correlation ID filtering, service-level faceting, structured property queries, and log-based alerting/dashboards.

Together with the existing `/health` endpoints (already aggregated by the gateway), this satisfies both the **logging** and **monitoring** parts of the criterion — Seq provides dashboards, alerts on log patterns (error rate spikes, slow requests), and signal detection.

## Scope

### 1. Seq container in docker-compose

Add a `seq` container (image: `datalust/seq:latest`, supports ARM64/Apple Silicon natively) to `docker-compose.yml`:
- Accept license via `ACCEPT_EULA=Y`
- Map host port `5341:5341` (ingestion) and `8080:80` (web UI)
- Add a named volume `seq-data` for persistence (add after `activity-db-data:` in volumes section)
- Place it in `winzy-network` alongside NATS and the databases
- Healthcheck using the built-in `seqcli` (the Seq image has NO `curl` or `wget`):
  ```yaml
  healthcheck:
    test: ["/seqsvr/Client/seqcli", "node", "health", "-s", "http://localhost"]
    interval: 15s
    timeout: 10s
    retries: 2
    start_period: 5s
  ```
- **Do NOT add `depends_on: seq` to application services.** Serilog's Seq sink is non-blocking — it buffers events in memory and retries silently if Seq is temporarily unavailable. Making services depend on Seq would mean a Seq failure takes down the entire application, which is an anti-pattern. Logging infrastructure must never block application startup.

### 2. Serilog packages in Winzy.Common

Add to `shared/Winzy.Common/Winzy.Common.csproj`:
- `Serilog.AspNetCore` (v10.0.0+ — includes `Serilog`, `Serilog.Extensions.Hosting`, `Serilog.Extensions.Logging`)
- `Serilog.Sinks.Seq`
- `Serilog.Enrichers.Environment` (for `MachineName` — returns container ID in Docker, useful for `docker ps` correlation)

Note: `Serilog.Enrichers.Thread` is intentionally excluded — in async ASP.NET Core, requests hop between threads, making thread IDs misleading rather than useful.

### 3. AddObservability() service extension in Winzy.Common

Add a new method in `ObservabilityExtensions.cs`:

```csharp
public static WebApplicationBuilder AddObservability(this WebApplicationBuilder builder, string serviceName)
```

**Important API choice:** Use `builder.Services.AddSerilog()` (on `IServiceCollection`), NOT the older `builder.Host.UseSerilog()` (on `IHostBuilder`). The `IHostBuilder` pattern is outdated. `AddSerilog()` scopes the logger to the DI container, which is critical for test safety — `WebApplicationFactory` creates a fresh DI container per test fixture, avoiding cross-test pollution through the global `Log.Logger` static.

The method should:
- Call `builder.Services.AddSerilog((services, config) => ...)` using the overload that provides access to `IServiceProvider`
- Configure `WriteTo.Seq(seqUrl)` where `seqUrl` comes from `builder.Configuration["Seq:Url"]` defaulting to `http://seq:5341`
- Configure `WriteTo.Console()` with the default human-readable output template (NOT `RenderedCompactJsonFormatter` — JSON on console is unreadable during local dev, and unnecessary since Seq receives structured events directly via HTTP)
- Enrich with: `Enrich.WithProperty("ServiceName", serviceName)`, `Enrich.WithMachineName()`
- Set minimum level from config: `ReadFrom.Configuration(builder.Configuration)`
- Overrides: `Microsoft.AspNetCore` → Warning, `Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Database.Command` → Warning, `NATS` → Warning
- Return `builder` for fluent chaining

### 4. Wire AddObservability in all 6 backend services

In each service's `Program.cs`, before `builder.Build()`:
```csharp
builder.AddObservability("habit-service");  // service name matches docker-compose service name
```

This replaces the default console logger. The existing `app.UseObservability()` call stays unchanged — it already wires correlation + request logging middleware, which will now flow through Serilog to Seq.

Affected files (add the `builder.AddObservability(...)` call):
- `services/auth-service/src/Program.cs` → `builder.AddObservability("auth-service")`
- `services/habit-service/src/Program.cs` → `builder.AddObservability("habit-service")`
- `services/social-service/src/Program.cs` → `builder.AddObservability("social-service")`
- `services/challenge-service/src/Program.cs` → `builder.AddObservability("challenge-service")`
- `services/notification-service/src/Program.cs` → `builder.AddObservability("notification-service")`
- `services/activity-service/src/Program.cs` → `builder.AddObservability("activity-service")`

### 5. Gateway — separate Serilog setup

The gateway **cannot reference Winzy.Common** (CLAUDE.md rule). Add Serilog packages directly to `services/gateway/src/Winzy.Gateway.csproj`:
- `Serilog.AspNetCore`
- `Serilog.Sinks.Seq`
- `Serilog.Enrichers.Environment`

Configure directly in `services/gateway/src/Program.cs` with the same Serilog pipeline as the shared extension, but inline. Use `builder.Services.AddSerilog(...)` with `ServiceName = "api-gateway"`.

### 6. Seq URL in docker-compose environment

Add `Seq__Url: http://seq:5341` to each service's environment block in `docker-compose.yml`. Also add to gateway.

### 7. appsettings.Development.json — local Seq URL

Add to each service's `appsettings.Development.json`:
```json
"Seq": {
  "Url": "http://localhost:5341"
}
```

This allows running services outside Docker during development while still sending logs to Seq running in Docker.

### 8. Verify correlation ID flow in Seq

After setup, verify in the Seq UI (`http://localhost:8080`):
- Filter by `CorrelationId` shows the full request chain (gateway → service → NATS subscriber)
- Filter by `ServiceName` isolates a single service
- The `UserId`, `Method`, `Path`, `StatusCode`, `ElapsedMs` properties from `RequestLoggingMiddleware` are searchable
- NATS subscriber logs include `NatsSubject`, `NatsConsumer`, `CorrelationId`

## Test impact

Adding Serilog to Winzy.Common means all service test projects transitively pull in Serilog. This is safe because:
- `builder.Services.AddSerilog()` scopes the logger to the DI container, which `WebApplicationFactory` creates fresh per test fixture — no cross-test pollution
- The Seq sink is non-blocking — if Seq is unreachable during tests, events are silently buffered and discarded. Tests never fail due to Seq being down.
- No changes to test fixtures are required
- Gateway tests are unaffected (no Winzy.Common dependency in their chain)

## What NOT to change

- **Do not modify `RequestLoggingMiddleware` or `CorrelationIdMiddleware`** — they already use `ILogger<T>` with structured templates. Serilog will capture their output automatically.
- **Do not modify any existing `_logger.Log*()` calls** — they already use named message templates that Serilog will parse as structured properties.
- **Do not add Serilog.RequestLogging middleware** (`app.UseSerilogRequestLogging()`) — we already have our own `RequestLoggingMiddleware`. Using both would double-log every request.
- **Do not remove the `"Logging"` section from appsettings.json** — Serilog's `ReadFrom.Configuration` can coexist, and it's useful as a fallback if Serilog bootstrap fails.
- **Do not use the static `Log.Logger`** — all configuration must go through `builder.Services.AddSerilog()` for test safety.

## Test plan

- `docker compose up` and open `http://localhost:8080` — Seq UI shows logs from all 7 services
- Register a user → search Seq for `CorrelationId` → see gateway log + auth-service log with same ID
- Complete a habit → see habit-service log + NATS subscriber logs in challenge/notification/activity services with same `CorrelationId`
- Filter by `ServiceName == "habit-service"` → only habit-service logs appear
- Stop Seq container → verify application services continue running and logging to console without errors
- Restart Seq → verify buffered events appear (if any were generated during the ~seconds of downtime)
- Run `dotnet test` on any service → verify tests pass without Seq running
- Run services outside Docker → verify logs go to console AND to `localhost:5341` if Seq is running

## Files to modify

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `docker-compose.yml` | Add `seq` container, `seq-data` volume, `Seq__Url` env vars on all 7 services |
| `shared/Winzy.Common/Winzy.Common.csproj` | Add 3 Serilog NuGet packages |
| `shared/Winzy.Common/Observability/ObservabilityExtensions.cs` | Add `AddObservability(this WebApplicationBuilder, string)` method |
| `services/auth-service/src/Program.cs` | Add `builder.AddObservability("auth-service")` |
| `services/habit-service/src/Program.cs` | Add `builder.AddObservability("habit-service")` |
| `services/social-service/src/Program.cs` | Add `builder.AddObservability("social-service")` |
| `services/challenge-service/src/Program.cs` | Add `builder.AddObservability("challenge-service")` |
| `services/notification-service/src/Program.cs` | Add `builder.AddObservability("notification-service")` |
| `services/activity-service/src/Program.cs` | Add `builder.AddObservability("activity-service")` |
| `services/gateway/src/Winzy.Gateway.csproj` | Add 3 Serilog NuGet packages |
| `services/gateway/src/Program.cs` | Add inline Serilog setup with `builder.Services.AddSerilog(...)` |
| `services/*/src/appsettings.Development.json` (7 files) | Add `Seq.Url` for local dev |

comments.author: Gabko14
comments.text:
## Fresh-eyes review — 3 corrections

### 1. CRITICAL: Remove `depends_on: seq` from all services

The bead says "All 7 application services should `depends_on: seq: condition: service_healthy`". This is an anti-pattern — if Seq fails to start, the **entire application is dead**. Logging infrastructure must never block application startup.

**Fix:** Do NOT add any `depends_on` for Seq on application services. Serilog's Seq sink handles transient Seq unavailability gracefully (buffers events in memory and retries). Services should start and run fine whether Seq is up or not.

### 2. Console sink should be human-readable, not CompactJSON

The bead says `WriteTo.Console(new RenderedCompactJsonFormatter())`. CompactJSON is unreadable on a terminal during local dev. Since Seq receives structured events directly via its HTTP ingestion API, there's no need for JSON on console.

**Fix:** Use `WriteTo.Console()` with the default human-readable output template. Structured properties are preserved in the Serilog pipeline regardless of console format — Seq still gets fully structured events.

### 3. Mention monitoring angle

The course criterion says "Log- **und Monitoring-Daten** werden an einen zentralisierten Dienst übertragen." The bead only covers logging. Should note that:
- Seq provides built-in **dashboards and alerts** on log patterns (error rate spikes, slow requests, etc.)
- The existing **/health** endpoints (already aggregated by the gateway) provide service-level health monitoring
- Together, Seq + health checks satisfy both the logging AND monitoring parts of the criterion

3. If 5xx or timeout → return 503 with message like 'Unable to verify friendship. Please try again.'

title: Notification redelivery can suppress push sends after the notification row is already saved
description:
Notification subscribers persist the notification row before calling `PushDeliveryService`, and on redelivery they return early when the idempotency key already exists. If the process crashes or throws after the DB save but before push delivery is fully finished, the redelivered message sees the saved row and skips push permanently.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Post-save failure/redelivery does not permanently suppress a push send that should still happen.
- The intended best-effort vs retry semantics for push are explicit in code and tests.
- Tests cover at least one failure/redelivery ordering around notification save vs push delivery.
close_reason: Notification subscribers retry push delivery on idempotency-duplicate detection

title: Concurrent challenge creation can bypass duplicate prevention
description:
PR #110 added duplicate prevention to POST /challenges, but the current implementation in services/challenge-service/src/Program.cs still performs a separate AnyAsync read before insert. Under concurrent retries or double-submits, two requests for the same creator/recipient/habit can both observe no active challenge and both insert, so duplicate active challenges are still possible.

## Why this matters
- The endpoint now promises duplicate prevention, but the guarantee is not atomic.
- Challenge Service acceptance criteria explicitly call for safe retry/idempotency behavior.
- The current migration only has non-unique indexes, so the database does not enforce this invariant.
- A naive partial unique index on status=Active is not enough on its own because expired challenges are only annotated as 'expired' at read time; they still remain Active in storage.

## Done when
- Duplicate prevention is enforced atomically for challenge creation.
- The chosen design still allows creating a new challenge after the previous one has genuinely expired.
- Integration coverage proves concurrent POST /challenges requests cannot create duplicate active challenges.
- Tests cover retry/double-submit behavior and document the expected conflict response.

## Context
Codex reported this on PR #110 review comment https://github.com/Gabko14/Winzy.ai/pull/110#discussion_r2921491142, and the issue is still present on feature/wave-2 as of 2026-03-12.
close_reason: Added unique filtered index (creator_id, recipient_id, habit_id) WHERE status='Active'. Endpoint expires stale Active challenges before insert. DbUpdateException on unique violation returns 409 Conflict. Concurrent requests proven safe by test.

title: Authenticated app shell: bottom tab navigation
description:
The current RootNavigator uses a flat state machine (useState<AppScreen>) with no visible navigation chrome. Users switch screens via callbacks, but there is no persistent tab bar, bottom navigation, or sidebar. This means:

- Friends screen (winzy.ai-78l) has no navigation entry point in the shell
- Activity feed (winzy.ai-uut) has no primary navigation surface
- Profile is only reachable via winzy.ai-3sc (wire navigation to profile)
- Settings (winzy.ai-412) has no shell entry

## Scope
- Add a bottom tab bar or equivalent primary navigation to the authenticated shell
- Required tabs: Today (home), Friends, Activity/Feed, Profile
- Notifications bell stays in the Today header (already wired)
- Keep the state machine clean: tab state + nested screen state (e.g. habit detail within Today tab, friend profile within Friends tab)
- Support web-responsive layout (tabs on mobile, possibly sidebar on desktop)

## Implementation Notes
- The RootNavigator state machine must evolve to support parallel tab state without losing the current screen-switching model
- Tab bar should show unread badge on Friends tab (pending requests) and Activity tab (new feed items) when those features land
- Must not break existing E2E tests that rely on Today screen being the default authenticated landing

## Quality Expectations
- Unit tests for tab navigation state transitions
- Playwright E2E test: authenticated user can switch between all tabs and return to Today
- Tab bar renders correctly on mobile web viewport
- Deep-link routing (e.g. from notification → friend profile) still works across tab boundaries

## Acceptance Criteria
- Authenticated users have a persistent, visible primary navigation surface with Today, Friends, Feed, and Profile tabs
- Tab switching preserves state within each tab (e.g. scrolling back to Today tab shows the same position)
- Existing navigation callbacks (onHabitPress, onNotifications) continue to work within the tab context
- E2E tests verify tab switching works and existing authenticated flows don't regress

title: Define privacy-sensitive invalidation strategy for visibility and friend changes
description:
Privacy-sensitive read models currently depend on Social visibility/friendship state, but the tracker does not yet make the invalidation path explicit. Decide and document how visibility changes and friend removal propagate to downstream consumers before those features land.

## Scope

- [ ] Cover public flame reads, activity-feed eligibility, and any other visibility-sensitive derived data that should not stay stale after friend removal or visibility changes.
- [ ] Define acceptable staleness, if any, and the user/privacy boundary it must respect.
- [ ] Define the logging and verification needed to catch privacy regressions.

## Affected Areas
- [ ] Public Flame Page (winzy.ai-szy)
- [ ] Activity Service / feed (winzy.ai-ssb, winzy.ai-uut)
- [ ] Social visibility authority and event contracts (winzy.ai-bdd, shared subjects/contracts)

## Notes
- This is a contract/architecture decision bead, not a request to weaken privacy for caching convenience.
- If new events are required, the tracker should name them explicitly rather than leaving them implied.
acceptance_criteria: The tracker explicitly defines how friend-removal and visibility-change invalidation reaches public/share/feed consumers, including any new events or synchronous re-check rules.\nAcceptable staleness, privacy boundaries, and observability requirements are documented well enough to catch regressions in tests and during rollout.\nDependent beads no longer rely on implicit cache magic for privacy-sensitive data freshness.

title: Unread notifications badge still drifts after failed mark-read and mark-all-read requests
description:
The notifications shell wiring now updates the global unread badge optimistically before the API succeeds. `useNotifications` rolls back the inbox items on failure, but the shell-level unread count is only decremented and never restored, so the badge can stay below server truth until the next poll.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Failed mark-read and mark-all-read calls leave the bell badge consistent with server truth.
- Tests cover badge rollback together with inbox rollback on failure.
- The notifications shell no longer relies on the next poll to repair local count drift.
close_reason: Merged in PR #113 (Wave 4)

comments.author: Gabko14
comments.text:
Audit 2026-03-13: Code review confirms the bug is real and partially addressed. Current state:
- RootNavigator passes onMarkAllRead={() => unreadCount.resetToZero()} (line 161)
- NotificationScreen calls onMarkAllRead() optimistically on mark-all-read
- On failure, it tries to restore with onUnreadCountChange?.(pageUnread)
- BUT: if onMarkAllRead was called (resets to 0), the rollback only adds back page-local unread count. If server has 50 unread but only 20 loaded on current page, failed mark-all-read leaves badge at 20 instead of 50.
- The 30-second poll (useUnreadCount.ts) is the only recovery mechanism for the gap.

FIX APPROACH: On mark-all-read failure, call unreadCount.refresh() (which polls server) instead of trying to manually restore a partial count. This guarantees server truth is restored immediately instead of waiting up to 30 seconds.

comments.text:
## Review Complete

### Adopted from Quote-Of-The-Day:
- **Non-Negotiables section** — project-specific hard rules that must never be violated
- **Commands section** — dev/build/test commands for quick reference
- **Git Workflow** — feature branches, naming conventions, PR flow

title: Gateway base config trusts an overly broad 172.16.0.0/12 proxy range
description: The forwarded-headers fix now loads ReverseProxy:TrustedProxies from configuration, but the checked-in base appsettings.json trusts the entire 172.16.0.0/12 range. Because appsettings.json applies outside local Docker too, any peer in that RFC1918 range can supply X-Forwarded-For and influence rate-limit partitioning. Narrow trust to environment-specific ingress proxies only; default should be empty/fail-safe.
acceptance_criteria: Checked-in gateway base config is fail-safe and does not trust broad private-network proxy ranges by default.\nTests cover empty/default trusted-proxy config, explicit trusted-proxy overrides, and spoofed forwarding headers from untrusted peers.\nDocs/examples make the intended local-Docker and real-ingress trusted-proxy setup explicit enough to avoid accidental re-broadening.

title: Challenge completion flow
description:
Handle challenge-completion celebration in the frontend. Backend detection logic lives in Challenge Service.

## Triggering The Celebration
- [ ] Celebration can be triggered when challenge completion is observed through the best available frontend signal.
- [ ] Push notifications may be one trigger path, but the celebration must also work when push is unavailable, denied, or delayed.
- [ ] Provide a fallback via status refetch, polling, or in-app event handling so web/PWA users still see completion.

- [ ] Warm, togetherness-focused reward framing
- [ ] Motion/animation hooks into the polish layer where appropriate

## Reward Claiming
- [ ] Users can see and claim/mark the shared reward through the relevant challenge surfaces.

## Quality Expectations
- [ ] Tests cover both push-present and no-push fallback trigger paths.
- [ ] User-visible logging/debug info is strong enough to diagnose why a celebration did or did not appear during E2E runs.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
Challenge completion celebration is not dependent on push notifications alone.

- Happy path: celebration overlay shows achievement context and reward description
- Happy path: user can claim/mark reward from celebration screen
- Edge case: celebration triggered via status refetch (no push) — poll/refetch detects completion

- Error condition: push denied/unavailable → fallback refetch still triggers celebration

- Verify celebration shows correct reward description and friend info
- Verify claim/mark button works and challenge status updates
- Test without push (intercept push subscription) → verify fallback works

title: Data export and account deletion
description:
Allow users to export their data and delete their account without manually dealing with individual services.

## Export Model
- [ ] One user-facing export action produces one documented bundled download through an orchestrated backend path (for example gateway- or auth-owned aggregation), not ad hoc client-side fan-out.
- [ ] Include the data owned by the in-scope services: Auth/profile data, habits/completions, friendships/visibility defaults, challenges, and any notification/activity data that remains in scope for this feature.
- [ ] JSON export is required; CSV is optional if it does not degrade fidelity.
- [ ] Download as file with clear completion/failure feedback.

## Delete My Account
- [ ] Confirmation dialog with warning
- [ ] Call Auth Service: DELETE /auth/account
- [ ] Auth Service publishes user.deleted event
- [ ] All in-scope services clean up their own user data on receiving the event
- [ ] Sign out and clear local session state
- [ ] Cannot be undone

## Orchestration And Verification
- [ ] Document the chosen export-bundle composition/orchestration model explicitly.
- [ ] Document how partial export or delayed deletion failures are surfaced and retried.
- [ ] Add end-to-end verification with strong correlation-friendly logging by user ID/request ID.

## Privacy
- [ ] Link to privacy policy in Settings
- [ ] Accessible from signup flow

## GDPR Considerations
- [ ] Right to access (export)
- [ ] Right to erasure (delete across all services)
- [ ] Data portability (export in common format)

## Notes
- Account deletion requires coordination across services via NATS.
- Export/delete should feel like one user action even though data ownership stays with the individual services.
acceptance_criteria:
A documented orchestration model exists for single-action export and account deletion without leaking service topology into the UI.
Each in-scope service has explicit export ownership and deletion-evidence expectations (per docs/decisions/export-contracts.md).

## Unit Tests (Backend — per service export endpoint)
- Happy path: GET /internal/export/{userId} returns correct JSON shape per export-contracts.md
- Happy path: export includes archived habits (user data, not just active)
- Edge case: export for user with no data returns valid empty structure
- Edge case: export for user with 10k+ completions handles pagination/memory
- Error condition: export endpoint for non-existent userId returns 404

## Unit Tests (Backend — deletion)
- Happy path: DELETE /auth/account publishes user.deleted and clears local data
- Happy path: each service user.deleted subscriber deletes all rows for userId
- Happy path: challenge service deletes ALL states (Active, Completed, Claimed, Cancelled)
- Edge case: user.deleted for already-deleted user is idempotent
- Edge case: service down during deletion → JetStream redelivers when it recovers

## Unit Tests (Frontend)
- Happy path: Export button triggers download with correct filename
- Happy path: Delete account shows confirmation dialog with warning
- Happy path: successful deletion signs out and clears session
- Error condition: export fails shows retry option
- Error condition: deletion fails shows try-again without logging user out

## E2E Tests (Playwright)
- Navigate to Settings → Account → Export my data → verify JSON file downloads
- Register new user → create habits → export → verify exported JSON contains the habits
- Delete account → verify redirect to sign-in → verify old credentials rejected
- Detailed step logging

title: Define per-service export contracts for bundled account export and deletion evidence
description:
The account export/delete feature already depends on multiple services, but the tracker still leaves each domain's export payload ownership and deletion-completion evidence too implicit. Define those contracts before the bundled orchestrator is implemented.

## Scope
- [ ] Allocate export payload ownership across Auth, Habit, Social, Challenge, and any derived Notification/Activity data that remains in scope.
- [ ] Decide explicitly whether derived notification/activity records are included in export, transformed, or excluded with rationale.
- [ ] Define the per-service evidence/logging needed to prove user.deleted cleanup finished or failed.
- [ ] Make the aggregator contract concrete enough that winzy.ai-7dh is not composing undefined payloads.

## Verification Expectations
- [ ] Define per-domain verification hooks or contract tests for export payload shape and deletion propagation.
- [ ] Make partial-failure diagnostics explicit so operators and tests can identify which service failed the export/delete workflow.

## Notes
- This is not the full implementation of export/delete; it is the planning/contract bead that prevents the feature from becoming hand-wavy cross-service glue.
- Preserve service ownership: each service exports and deletes only its own data.

title: NATS Setup & Event Contracts
description:
Set up NATS messaging and define shared event contracts.

## NATS Server
- [ ] Add NATS container to docker-compose.yml
- [ ] Configure JetStream for persistent messaging — REQUIRED from day 1 (not optional)
- [ ] Expose management port for monitoring

## Shared Contracts (Winzy.Contracts project)
- [ ] Define C# record types for all events:
  - UserRegisteredEvent { Guid UserId, string Username }
  - UserDeletedEvent { Guid UserId }
  - HabitCreatedEvent { Guid UserId, Guid HabitId, string Name }
  - HabitCompletedEvent { Guid UserId, Guid HabitId, DateTime Date, double Consistency }
  - FriendRequestSentEvent { Guid FromUserId, Guid ToUserId }
  - FriendRequestAcceptedEvent { Guid UserId1, Guid UserId2 }
  - ChallengeCreatedEvent { Guid ChallengeId, Guid FromUser, Guid ToUser, Guid HabitId }
  - ChallengeCompletedEvent { Guid ChallengeId, Guid UserId, string Reward }

## NATS Client Helper (Winzy.Common project)
- [ ] NatsPublisher service for publishing events
- [ ] NatsSubscriber base class for consuming events
- [ ] JSON serialization configuration
- [ ] Connection string from environment variable
- [ ] Reconnection handling

## Subject Naming Convention
- user.registered
- user.deleted
- habit.created
- habit.completed
- friend.request.sent
- friend.request.accepted
- challenge.created
- challenge.completed

Note: No `events.` prefix — subjects match the event names directly. This aligns with KONZEPTDOKUMENT.md.

## Notes
- All services reference Winzy.Contracts for type-safe events
- Use NATS JetStream from day 1 — NOT core NATS. user.deleted must reach all 5 subscribers with at-least-once delivery guarantees. Core NATS is fire-and-forget: a service restarting during that event silently skips cleanup, causing orphaned data and GDPR violations.
- user.deleted triggers cascade cleanup in all services (Winzy.ai-7dh)
close_reason: Merged PR #94

title: Review & improve beads usage — discover unused features
description:
Meta-review of our beads setup. We're using beads for issue tracking, but there are likely powerful features we're not taking advantage of yet.

## Core Question
What useful beads features exist that we're not utilizing?

## Known Gaps to Investigate
- [ ] **Milestones** — Group beads into milestones (e.g., "MVP launch", "P2 social features") for tracking progress toward goals
- [ ] **Labels / Tags** — Categorize beads beyond issue_type (e.g., "frontend", "backend", "infra", "design")
- [ ] **Blocking / Dependencies** — Formally link beads that block each other (some descriptions reference rs-XXX but is there native support?)
- [ ] **Sprints / Iterations** — Time-boxed work planning if supported
- [ ] **Custom fields** — Any additional metadata fields we could use
- [ ] **Views / Filters** — Saved views for common queries (e.g., "all P1 frontend tasks")
- [ ] **Automations** — Auto-close, auto-assign, status transitions

## Actions
- [ ] Read the beads docs thoroughly — check what features exist
- [ ] Review our .beads/config.yaml — are there config options we're missing?
- [ ] Check if beadspace dashboard has untapped capabilities
- [ ] Set up milestones for our project phases (MVP, P2 Social, P3 Polish)
- [ ] Document any new features we adopt in CLAUDE.md

## Why This Matters
Better tooling = better project management = faster shipping. We should use the tools we already have to their full potential before adding new ones.

title: API Gateway (YARP)
description:
Implement the API Gateway using YARP reverse proxy.

- [ ] /habits/* → habit-service:5002

- [ ] /challenges/* → challenge-service:5004

- [ ] Extract user_id from JWT claims
- [ ] Inject X-User-Id header for downstream services
- [ ] Allow unauthenticated access to /auth/register and /auth/login

## Features
- [ ] Rate limiting per client/IP
- [ ] CORS configuration for frontend
- [ ] Health check aggregation (GET /health)
- [ ] Request/response logging

- [ ] BLOCK /habits/user/* — internal-only, never expose through Gateway

title: Notifications inbox feature is closed but not wired into the authenticated app shell
description:

- Playwright coverage exercises the user-visible UI flow rather than only the backend API contract.

title: Backend Coding Guidelines
description:
Define .NET backend coding conventions for CLAUDE.md.

## Code Style
- [ ] .editorconfig with C# conventions
- [ ] PascalCase for public members, camelCase for locals
- [ ] File-scoped namespaces
- [ ] Primary constructors where appropriate

## Project Structure (per service)
- Program.cs — Service configuration and endpoint mapping
- Endpoints/ — Minimal API endpoint groups
- Entities/ — EF Core entity classes
- Services/ — Business logic
- Data/ — DbContext and migrations

## Patterns
- [ ] Minimal API endpoint groups (static classes with MapGroup)
- [ ] Dependency injection for all services
- [ ] Result pattern for error handling (no exceptions for business logic)
- [ ] Strongly-typed configuration (IOptions pattern)

## API Conventions
- [ ] REST resource naming (plural nouns)
- [ ] user_id from X-User-Id header (set by Gateway)
- [ ] Pagination with cursor-based approach

## Standard API Response Format
- [ ] Success: { data: T } or { data: T[], cursor: string }
- [ ] Error: { error: string, details?: string[] }
- [ ] HTTP status codes: 200 (OK), 201 (Created), 204 (No Content), 400 (Bad Request), 401 (Unauthorized), 404 (Not Found), 422 (Validation), 500 (Server Error)

## Cross-Service Response Contracts
- [ ] GET /habits/user/{userId} returns: { data: [{ id, name, icon, color, frequency, consistency, visibility }] }
- [ ] GET /friends (Social → called by Activity Service): { data: [{ friendId }] }
- [ ] Document response shapes in shared/Winzy.Contracts/ alongside event types

## Testing Requirements (Non-Negotiable)
- [ ] Every endpoint: at least 1 integration test (Testcontainers + real PostgreSQL)
- [ ] Every business logic method: unit tests covering happy path + edge cases
- [ ] Every NATS handler: test that correct events trigger correct actions
- [ ] Coverage target: 80%+ per service
- [ ] Test naming: MethodName_Scenario_ExpectedResult

## Non-Negotiables
- [ ] No code merged without tests
- [ ] No direct DB access across service boundaries
- [ ] All inter-service communication via REST or NATS
- [ ] All endpoints require authentication except: /auth/register, /auth/login, /auth/refresh, /habits/public/{username}

## Notes
- These guidelines go into CLAUDE.md for AI-assisted development
- Keep it practical, not academic

title: Social Service Implementation
description:
Implement the Social Service (.NET Minimal API + PostgreSQL).

## Endpoints
- [ ] POST /friends/request — Send friend request
- [ ] PUT /friends/request/{id}/accept — Accept request
- [ ] PUT /friends/request/{id}/decline — Decline request
- [ ] DELETE /friends/{id} — Remove friend
- [ ] GET /friends — List friends
- [ ] GET /friends/requests — List pending requests (incoming + outgoing)
- [ ] GET /friends/{id}/profile — Friend profile + visible habits
- [ ] PUT /friends/visibility/{habitId} — Set habit visibility
- [ ] GET/PUT social privacy preferences needed for default visibility on new habits

## Database Schema (social_db)
- friendships table: id, user_id, friend_id, status (pending/accepted), created_at
- visibility_settings table: id, user_id, habit_id, visibility (private/friends/public)
- social_preferences table (or equivalent): per-user defaults such as default habit visibility

## Cross-Service Communication
- [ ] Friend profile composition includes Auth-owned identity data (display name, avatar, member-since or equivalent public profile metadata).
- [ ] GET /friends/{id}/profile uses Habit Service habit data plus Social visibility rules.
- [ ] Social Service is the source of truth for who can see which habits and what the default sharing posture is.
- [ ] Public/profile read models that depend on visibility must consume Social's rules rather than duplicating their own source of truth.

## NATS Events
- [ ] Publish friend.request.sent { fromUserId, toUserId }
- [ ] Publish friend.request.accepted { userId1, userId2 }
- [ ] Subscribe user.deleted → remove all friendships, visibility settings, and social preferences for userId

## Notes
- Friendship is bidirectional (both users see each other)
- Visibility defaults to private for new habits unless the user's explicit social preference says otherwise
- Social Service is the authority on who can see what

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria: Friendship, visibility, default-sharing preference, and profile-composition behavior work end-to-end with explicit internal-service and gateway-facing social route contracts.\nTests cover friendship state transitions, visibility enforcement, profile composition, user.deleted cleanup, and whichever invalidation strategy the tracker adopts for visibility/friend changes.\nStructured logs make social mutations, profile reads, privacy-sensitive cache/visibility paths, and failure reasons diagnosable by user IDs, relationship IDs, and visibility state.
notes:
## Done When
- [ ] All 7 endpoints return correct status codes
- [ ] Friendship is bidirectional (both users see each other)
- [ ] Visibility defaults to private for new habits
- [ ] Cross-service call to Habit Service works (GET /habits/user/{userId})

- [ ] 80%+ code coverage

title: Auth Service Implementation
description:
Implement the Auth Service (.NET Minimal API + PostgreSQL).

## Endpoints
- [ ] POST /auth/register — Create account (hash password with Argon2id)
- [ ] POST /auth/login — Validate credentials, return JWT + refresh token
- [ ] POST /auth/refresh — Exchange refresh token for new JWT
- [ ] POST /auth/logout — Invalidate the current refresh token and clear the web refresh cookie/session bootstrap state
- [ ] GET /auth/profile — Get own profile
- [ ] PUT /auth/profile — Update name, avatar
- [ ] PUT /auth/password — Change password
- [ ] DELETE /auth/account — Delete account (publishes user.deleted event)
- [ ] GET /auth/users/search?q={query} — Search users by username/email (for friend discovery)

## Database Schema (auth_db)
- users table: id (UUID), username (unique, URL-safe, required), email, password_hash, display_name, avatar_url, created_at
- refresh_tokens table: id, user_id, token, expires_at, revoked_at

## JWT Configuration
- [ ] Access token: short-lived (15 min)
- [ ] Refresh token: long-lived (7 days)
- [ ] Sign with secret key (from environment variable)
- [ ] Include user_id and email in claims

## NATS Events
- [ ] Publish user.registered { userId, username } on registration
- [ ] Publish user.deleted { userId } on account deletion

## Security
- [ ] Argon2id password hashing (replaces BCrypt — memory-hard, GPU/ASIC resistant, OWASP recommended for new projects)
- [ ] Validate email format
- [ ] Rate limiting on login attempts
- [ ] Refresh token rotation
- [ ] User search returns limited fields only (id, displayName, avatarUrl)

## Token Storage Strategy (Web PWA + Native)
- [ ] Access token: in-memory only (JS variable, 15 min TTL) — never written to storage, immune to XSS
- [ ] Refresh token (web): HttpOnly + SameSite=Strict cookie via Set-Cookie response header
- [ ] Refresh token (native): expo-secure-store (OK on native, XSS is not a concern there)
- [ ] On app load: call POST /auth/refresh to hydrate in-memory access token from cookie/store
- [ ] POST /auth/login response sets cookie on web AND returns refresh token in body for native
- [ ] POST /auth/refresh clears old cookie and sets new one (rotation)
- [ ] POST /auth/logout clears HttpOnly cookie server-side and invalidates the corresponding refresh token/session

**Why:** expo-secure-store silently falls back to localStorage on web, making the refresh token XSS-stealable. HttpOnly cookies cannot be read by JS at all.

## Notes
- Profile data (name, avatar) also lives here
- Other services identify users by user_id from JWT (injected by Gateway)
- Password reset flow (forgot password → email → reset) deferred to post-MVP
acceptance_criteria:
Registration, login, refresh, logout, profile, and user-search behaviors work end-to-end with the intended web/native session model.
Tests cover password hashing, username validation/collision handling, refresh rotation, logout cookie/token invalidation, and account-deletion event publication.
Logs and failure diagnostics make auth/session failures understandable without exposing secrets, raw credentials, or token material.
notes:
## Done When
- [ ] All 9 endpoints return correct status codes
- [ ] JWT generation and validation works (access + refresh tokens)
- [ ] Logout invalidates the relevant refresh token/session and clears the web cookie path correctly
- [ ] Argon2id password hashing implemented
- [ ] user.registered event publishes via NATS
- [ ] user.deleted event publishes via NATS
- [ ] User search returns limited fields only
- [ ] GET /health responds with DB + NATS status
- [ ] Dockerfile builds and runs in Docker Compose
