title: Witness Links — private revocable flame pages for one-person accountability
description:
Allow a user to create a private, revocable flame link for one real person (partner, sibling, coach, friend) without requiring the viewer to create a Winzy account. This is not the public flame page and not the friends graph. It is a low-friction accountability surface: "here is my flame if you want to keep an eye on it."

## Core Concept
- Owner creates a witness link with an optional private label like "Maya" or "Coach Sam"
- Link is an opaque high-entropy token, not a guessable username route
- Viewer opens the link anonymously and sees a witness-safe flame page
- Owner can revoke or rotate any link at any time
- Possession of the link is the access model in v1; no witness signup required

## Scope And Boundary
- Witness links are NOT public profile aliases and must not be indexable or searchable
- Witness links are NOT a chat, comments, nudges, likes, or notifications feature
- Witness links should expose only the habits intentionally selected for that link
- Use a per-link habit allowlist owned by Social visibility authority rather than turning witness sharing into global public visibility
- Revocation must take effect quickly enough that a stale copied link does not keep working indefinitely

## Owner Experience
- Create and manage witness links from an existing profile, settings, or share surface
- Optional private label for the intended witness
- Choose which habits are visible through that link
- Copy/share link
- View active links and revoke individually

## Viewer Experience
- Route like `/w/{token}` renders a lightweight flame page without auth
- Page shows owner identity, selected habit flames, and supportive copy
- Keep CTA to create an account subtle; this surface is accountability first, marketing second
- Unknown or revoked token renders a clear not-available state without leaking whether the owner exists

## Architecture Notes
- Social Service remains the authority on witness-link access and per-link habit allowlists
- Public flame page and witness links should share rendering patterns where possible, but use distinct contracts and caching rules
- Logs and analytics must never store full token values; log link ID, owner ID, and outcome instead
- Rate limiting and anti-scraping rules must be explicit

## Quality Expectations
- Backend tests cover token creation, anonymous lookup, allowlist filtering, revoke/rotate behavior, archived/private habit changes, and safe handling of invalid tokens
- Frontend tests cover owner create/manage UI, anonymous viewer states, and supportive copy
- Playwright covers create link -> open anonymously -> revoke -> verify link stops working, with detailed step logging
acceptance_criteria:
Users can create, share, and revoke private witness links that expose only the intended habits to anonymous viewers without requiring viewer accounts.
Witness pages are unguessable, non-indexed, privacy-safe, and stop working promptly after revocation without leaking whether the owner account exists.
Tests cover owner management, anonymous viewing, habit-selection changes, revoked/invalid tokens, and logging/rate-limiting boundaries well enough that privacy regressions are catchable before release.
notes:
## Non-Goals
- No witness account creation or identity verification in v1
- No comments, reactions, chat, reminders, or witness-triggered nudges
- No browseable directory, discovery surface, or username fallback route
- Do not replace or dilute the existing public flame page; this is a separate private-sharing surface

## Suggested Verification
### Backend Unit / Integration
- Happy path: create witness link with two selected habits and anonymous viewer sees only those habits
- Happy path: revoke link and existing URL immediately stops resolving
- Happy path: rotate link invalidates old token and preserves owner-managed metadata as intended
- Edge case: archived habit disappears from witness page without breaking the rest of the page
- Edge case: habit removed from link allowlist no longer appears on subsequent anonymous loads
- Edge case: same owner maintains multiple witness links with different habit selections
- Error condition: invalid or malformed token returns the same non-leaky not-available response as revoked token
- Error condition: excessive anonymous requests hit an explicit rate limit path

### Frontend Unit / Component
- Happy path: owner can create link, name it, choose habits, and copy it
- Happy path: witness page renders supportive copy and selected flame cards without auth chrome
- Edge case: owner with no selected habits gets a clear empty-state prompt instead of a broken link
- Error condition: create/revoke failure preserves owner context and shows recoverable feedback

### E2E Tests (Playwright)
- Authenticated owner creates a witness link and opens it in an anonymous browser context
- Anonymous viewer sees owner identity plus only the selected habits
- Owner revokes the link and anonymous refresh now shows not-available state
- Owner edits habit selection and anonymous page updates accordingly
- Detailed step logging for creation, copy/share, anonymous open, and revocation flows

title: Flame Promises — visible, time-boxed self-commitments tied to habit flames
description:
Let a user make a visible, time-boxed promise tied to one habit's flame. This is a self-authored commitment, not a challenge from another person. The product value is not competition or rewards; it is making intention socially legible in the same supportive, consistency-first language as the rest of Winzy.

## Core Concept
- A promise belongs to one owner and one habit
- The owner sets a target and an end date
- The owner can keep the promise private or expose it on eligible flame surfaces
- The promise is shown as a concise, system-generated statement such as "Keeping reading above 70% through April 30"
- When the period ends, the promise resolves into supportive history instead of a punitive failure state

## V1 Promise Model
To keep the first version sharp and implementable, v1 should ship one clear promise type:
- Maintain this habit at or above a chosen consistency threshold until a chosen end date

This deliberately reuses Winzy's existing consistency philosophy instead of introducing streaks or a large menu of goal types on day one. If the idea proves valuable, later extensions can add more promise shapes.

## Why This Is Distinct
- NOT a challenge: no inviter, no recipient, no reward claim, no friend-validation flow
- NOT a social post: public-facing copy should be generated from structured fields, not arbitrary free-form status text
- NOT a streak pledge: promises must stay consistency-based and supportive
- NOT a punishment loop: missing a promise should archive it with reflective, non-shaming framing

## Owner Experience
- Create promise from Habit Detail as the primary surface
- Choose target consistency threshold and end date
- Optional private note for the owner only, if helpful, but the shareable promise statement remains generated
- View the active promise inline with current progress toward keeping it
- End, archive, or replace the active promise intentionally
- Keep v1 simple by allowing at most one active promise per habit

## Viewer / Share Surface Experience
- If the owner opts in, an active promise can appear on existing flame surfaces where that habit is already visible
- Public flame exposure only works when the underlying habit is already public
- Future witness-link exposure should only work when the link includes that habit; promises must never widen visibility beyond the habit's existing sharing rules
- Promise display should feel calm and legible: statement, time horizon, current keep/on-track state

## Product Behavior
- Promise progress should be derived from the same underlying consistency contract the flame already uses
- Promise state language should stay supportive: "on track", "kept", "ended below promise" or equivalent non-punitive phrasing
- When a promise ends, preserve it as history instead of erasing the attempt
- Visibility changes for the underlying habit must remove or narrow promise exposure quickly enough to avoid privacy drift

## Architecture Notes
- Habit/consistency math should remain the source of truth for promise evaluation; do not invent a second interpretation of consistency
- Promise visibility can only be equal to or narrower than the underlying habit's visibility
- Do not implement this by simply rebranding Challenge entities in the UI; shared calculation primitives are fine, but the product meaning and data contract should stay self-owned
- Logs and analytics must avoid storing any owner-private notes in public-surface events unless explicitly intended

## Quality Expectations
- Backend tests cover promise creation, evaluation, closure, replacement rules, and visibility-safe projection to public/share surfaces
- Frontend tests cover owner creation/manage flows, progress rendering, supportive copy, and end-of-promise states
- Playwright covers create promise -> observe on owner surface -> observe on public surface when enabled -> end or revoke visibility -> verify disappearance/update with detailed step logging
acceptance_criteria:
Users can create and track a structured, time-boxed Flame Promise for a habit without turning it into a challenge, streak, or social-post feature.
Active promises reuse the existing consistency contract and can appear only on flame surfaces where the underlying habit is already visible, with privacy-safe invalidation when visibility narrows.
Tests cover owner creation and lifecycle management, share-surface projection, end-state behavior, and non-punitive copy well enough that product and privacy regressions are catchable before release.
notes:
## Non-Goals
- No arbitrary public free-text promises in v1
- No friend acceptance, witness confirmation, likes, comments, reactions, or messaging
- No streak-based targets or "X days in a row" framing
- No replacement of the existing Challenge feature; this is self-commitment, not a social reward flow
- No promise type explosion in v1; start with one strong consistency-threshold promise shape

## Suggested Verification
### Backend Unit / Integration
- Happy path: create active promise with threshold + end date for a habit and compute current on-track state from the shared consistency contract
- Happy path: completed promise archives as kept when the threshold is satisfied at the end date
- Happy path: replacing an active promise for the same habit follows the intended one-active-promise rule without destroying history
- Edge case: habit visibility narrows from public to private and promise disappears from public projection promptly
- Edge case: archived habit closes or hides active promise gracefully without broken history records
- Edge case: promise end date passes while consistency is below target and the promise resolves into supportive ended history, not a failed hard-stop state
- Error condition: invalid threshold/date combinations are rejected with clear validation data
- Error condition: anonymous public request never reveals private promise data for a non-public habit

### Frontend Unit / Component
- Happy path: owner creates promise from Habit Detail and sees generated promise statement plus current keep/on-track state
- Happy path: owner can archive or replace a promise intentionally
- Edge case: supportive copy appears for active, kept, and ended-below-promise states without shame-oriented language
- Edge case: owner cannot opt promise into a public surface when the habit itself is not eligible
- Error condition: create/update failure preserves form state and shows recoverable feedback
- Verification: no streak language appears anywhere in promise UI

### E2E Tests (Playwright)
- Authenticated owner creates a Flame Promise from Habit Detail
- Promise appears on the owner surface with generated statement and progress state
- If enabled and the habit is public, the promise appears on the public flame page
- Owner disables public visibility or changes habit visibility and the public promise disappears
- Promise reaches end state and renders supportive kept or ended-below-promise history
- Detailed step logging for creation, exposure, visibility changes, and end-state verification

title: Docker Compose health checks and service boot order
description:
Prevent services from crashing on startup because PostgreSQL or NATS isn't ready yet.

## Problem
Without health checks and `depends_on: condition: service_healthy`, .NET services try to
connect to their database the moment Docker starts them — before PostgreSQL has finished
initialising. This causes startup crashes and requires manual `docker compose restart`.

## PostgreSQL Health Checks
Add to every Postgres container in docker-compose.yml:
```yaml
healthcheck:
  test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U postgres -d ${DB_NAME}"]
  interval: 5s
  timeout: 5s
  retries: 10
  start_period: 10s
```

## NATS Health Check
```yaml
healthcheck:
  test: ["CMD-SHELL", "wget -qO- http://localhost:8222/healthz || exit 1"]
  interval: 5s
  timeout: 5s
  retries: 10
```

## Service Boot Order
Every .NET service should declare:
```yaml
depends_on:
  {service}-db:
    condition: service_healthy
  nats:
    condition: service_healthy
```

## Checklist
- [ ] healthcheck added to all 6 PostgreSQL containers (auth-db, habit-db, social-db, challenge-db, notification-db, activity-db)
- [ ] healthcheck added to NATS container
- [ ] All 7 .NET services have depends_on with service_healthy conditions
- [ ] Tested: docker compose up from scratch boots all services without crashes
- [ ] Tested: docker compose up after docker compose down (data persisted)
close_reason: Already implemented in docker-compose.yml — all health checks and depends_on conditions are in place, verified by smoke test

title: HTTP test files (.http) for all API endpoints
description:
## Problem

The microservice functionality is verified by ~564 xUnit integration tests and 11 Playwright E2E specs, but there are no manual API test files (Postman, Bruno, or .http) in the project. The course criterion requires: "Funktionalität der Microservices durch Tests (Postman/Bruno) überprüft."

## Goal

Create **.http files** (VS Code REST Client / JetBrains HTTP Client format) that cover every public and authenticated endpoint in the system. These files serve as:
1. **Living API documentation** — shows exactly how to call each endpoint with realistic payloads
2. **Manual verification tool** — one-click execution from the IDE
3. **Course criterion fulfillment** — equivalent to Postman/Bruno but versionable and lightweight

## Structure

Create a top-level \`http/\` directory with one .http file per service, plus a shared environment file:

\`\`\`
http/
├── http-client.env.json       # Environment variables (base URL, tokens)
├── auth.http                  # Auth service endpoints
├── habits.http                # Habit service endpoints
├── social.http                # Social service endpoints
├── challenges.http            # Challenge service endpoints
├── notifications.http         # Notification service endpoints
├── activity.http              # Activity service endpoints
└── health.http                # Health + infrastructure checks
\`\`\`

## Environment file (http-client.env.json)

\`\`\`json
{
  "dev": {
    "baseUrl": "http://localhost:5050",
    "accessToken": "<paste-after-login>",
    "testEmail": "test@example.com",
    "testUsername": "testuser",
    "testPassword": "TestPassword123!",
    "friendId": "<paste-friend-guid>",
    "habitId": "<paste-habit-guid>",
    "challengeId": "<paste-challenge-guid>",
    "notificationId": "<paste-notification-guid>"
  }
}
\`\`\`

## Endpoint coverage per file

### auth.http (10 requests)

| # | Method | Path | Auth | Request body |
|---|--------|------|------|-------------|
| 1 | POST | /auth/register | Public | \`{ "email", "username", "password", "displayName" }\` |
| 2 | POST | /auth/login | Public | \`{ "emailOrUsername", "password" }\` |
| 3 | POST | /auth/refresh | Public | \`{ "refreshToken" }\` |
| 4 | POST | /auth/logout | Bearer | empty or \`{ "refreshToken" }\` |
| 5 | GET | /auth/profile | Bearer | — |
| 6 | PUT | /auth/profile | Bearer | \`{ "displayName", "avatarUrl" }\` |
| 7 | PUT | /auth/password | Bearer | \`{ "currentPassword", "newPassword" }\` |
| 8 | GET | /auth/users/search?q=test | Bearer | — |
| 9 | GET | /auth/export | Bearer | — |
| 10 | DELETE | /auth/account | Bearer | — (put last — destructive) |

**Flow**: Run register → copy token from response → use in subsequent requests. Login is an alternative entry point.

### habits.http (11 requests)

| # | Method | Path | Auth | Request body / notes |
|---|--------|------|------|---------------------|
| 1 | POST | /habits | Bearer | \`{ "name": "Morning Run", "icon": "🏃", "frequency": "daily" }\` |
| 2 | POST | /habits | Bearer | \`{ "name": "Read Book", "icon": "📖", "frequency": "weekly" }\` |
| 3 | POST | /habits | Bearer | \`{ "name": "Gym", "icon": "💪", "frequency": "custom", "customDays": ["Monday","Wednesday","Friday"] }\` |
| 4 | GET | /habits | Bearer | — |
| 5 | GET | /habits/{{habitId}} | Bearer | — |
| 6 | PUT | /habits/{{habitId}} | Bearer | \`{ "name": "Evening Run", "color": "#FF5733" }\` |
| 7 | POST | /habits/{{habitId}}/complete | Bearer | \`{ "timezone": "Europe/Vienna" }\` |
| 8 | POST | /habits/{{habitId}}/complete | Bearer | \`{ "date": "2026-03-15", "timezone": "Europe/Vienna" }\` — backfill |
| 9 | DELETE | /habits/{{habitId}}/completions/2026-03-15 | Bearer | — undo completion |
| 10 | GET | /habits/{{habitId}}/stats | Bearer | Header: \`X-Timezone: Europe/Vienna\` |
| 11 | GET | /habits/completions?date=2026-03-18 | Bearer | — today's completions |
| 12 | GET | /habits/public/{{testUsername}} | Public | — public flame page |
| 13 | GET | /habits/public/{{testUsername}}/flame.svg | Public | — SVG badge |
| 14 | DELETE | /habits/{{habitId}} | Bearer | — archive (put last) |

### social.http (11 requests)

| # | Method | Path | Auth | Request body / notes |
|---|--------|------|------|---------------------|
| 1 | POST | /social/friends/request | Bearer | \`{ "friendId": "{{friendId}}" }\` |
| 2 | GET | /social/friends/requests | Bearer | — incoming + outgoing |
| 3 | GET | /social/friends/requests/count | Bearer | — pending count |
| 4 | PUT | /social/friends/request/{{requestId}}/accept | Bearer | — (requires second user's token) |
| 5 | PUT | /social/friends/request/{{requestId}}/decline | Bearer | — |
| 6 | GET | /social/friends?page=1&pageSize=10 | Bearer | — paginated list |
| 7 | GET | /social/friends/{{friendId}}/profile | Bearer | — friend's visible habits |
| 8 | PUT | /social/visibility/{{habitId}} | Bearer | \`{ "visibility": "friends" }\` |
| 9 | GET | /social/visibility | Bearer | — all visibility settings |
| 10 | GET | /social/preferences | Bearer | — default visibility |
| 11 | PUT | /social/preferences | Bearer | \`{ "defaultHabitVisibility": "friends" }\` |
| 12 | DELETE | /social/friends/{{friendId}} | Bearer | — remove (put last) |

### challenges.http (5 requests)

| # | Method | Path | Auth | Request body / notes |
|---|--------|------|------|---------------------|
| 1 | POST | /challenges | Bearer | \`{ "habitId", "recipientId", "milestoneType": "consistencyTarget", "targetValue": 80, "periodDays": 30, "rewardDescription": "Coffee at Starbucks ☕" }\` |
| 2 | POST | /challenges | Bearer | \`{ "milestoneType": "daysInPeriod", "targetValue": 20, "periodDays": 30, ... }\` — second variant |
| 3 | GET | /challenges?page=1&pageSize=10 | Bearer | — |
| 4 | GET | /challenges/{{challengeId}} | Bearer | — detail with progress |
| 5 | PUT | /challenges/{{challengeId}}/claim | Bearer | — claim completed challenge |
| 6 | DELETE | /challenges/{{challengeId}} | Bearer | — cancel (put last) |

### notifications.http (7 requests)

| # | Method | Path | Auth | Request body / notes |
|---|--------|------|------|---------------------|
| 1 | GET | /notifications/vapid-public-key | Public | — VAPID key for web push |
| 2 | GET | /notifications?page=1&pageSize=10 | Bearer | — |
| 3 | GET | /notifications/unread-count | Bearer | — |
| 4 | PUT | /notifications/{{notificationId}}/read | Bearer | — mark single read |
| 5 | PUT | /notifications/read-all | Bearer | — mark all read |
| 6 | PUT | /notifications/settings | Bearer | \`{ "habitReminders": true, "friendActivity": true, "challengeUpdates": true }\` |
| 7 | POST | /notifications/devices | Bearer | \`{ "platform": "web_push", "token": "test-push-token", "deviceId": "test-device-1" }\` |
| 8 | DELETE | /notifications/devices | Bearer | \`{ "deviceId": "test-device-1" }\` |

### activity.http (1 request)

| # | Method | Path | Auth | Notes |
|---|--------|------|------|-------|
| 1 | GET | /activity/feed?limit=20 | Bearer | — first page |
| 2 | GET | /activity/feed?cursor={{nextCursor}}&limit=20 | Bearer | — pagination |

### health.http (2 requests)

| # | Method | Path | Auth | Notes |
|---|--------|------|------|-------|
| 1 | GET | /health | Public | — aggregated gateway health |
| 2 | GET | http://localhost:4222/healthz | Public | — NATS health (direct, not via gateway) |

## Format conventions

Each .http file should follow this pattern:

\`\`\`http
### Register a new user
POST {{baseUrl}}/auth/register
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "email": "{{testEmail}}",
  "username": "{{testUsername}}",
  "password": "{{testPassword}}",
  "displayName": "Test User"
}

###

### Login
POST {{baseUrl}}/auth/login
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "emailOrUsername": "{{testEmail}}",
  "password": "{{testPassword}}"
}
\`\`\`

Rules:
- **\`###\` separators** between requests (standard .http format)
- **Descriptive comment** above each request explaining what it does
- **\`{{variable}}\`** syntax for environment variables (compatible with both VS Code REST Client and JetBrains)
- **Realistic payloads** — not placeholder garbage. Use "Morning Run", "Europe/Vienna", actual enum values
- **Logical ordering** — CRUD order (create → read → update → use → delete). Destructive operations last.
- **Comments for flow dependencies** — e.g., "# Copy accessToken from the login response above"
- **Both happy path and edge case requests** where useful (e.g., duplicate registration, invalid email)

## What NOT to do

- **Do not include internal endpoints** (/internal/*, /habits/user/{userId}) — they are blocked by the gateway's InternalRouteBlockMiddleware and cannot be reached through the gateway
- **Do not hardcode tokens** — use {{accessToken}} variable
- **Do not create Postman collections or Bruno files** — .http is the chosen format
- **Do not auto-generate** — write each request manually with meaningful test data and descriptive comments

## Test plan

- Open each .http file in VS Code with REST Client extension (or JetBrains Rider)
- Run register → login → copy token → set in env
- Execute requests top to bottom in each file
- Verify each returns expected status code and response shape
- Verify public endpoints work without Bearer token
- Verify authenticated endpoints return 401 without Bearer token

comments.author: Gabko14
comments.text:
## Fresh-eyes review — 3 corrections

### 1. Section header counts are wrong

Every section header lists the wrong request count compared to the actual table rows:
- "habits.http (11 requests)" → actually **14 rows**
- "social.http (11 requests)" → actually **12 rows**
- "challenges.http (5 requests)" → actually **6 rows**
- "notifications.http (7 requests)" → actually **8 rows**
- "activity.http (1 request)" → actually **2 rows**
- auth.http (10) and health.http (2) are correct

**Fix:** Use the table row counts as the source of truth. The tables themselves are correct — only the headers are stale.

### 2. NATS health endpoint uses wrong port

health.http says `GET http://localhost:4222/healthz`. Port 4222 is the NATS **client** port. The monitoring/health port is **8222** (configured via `-m 8222` in docker-compose).

Additionally, port 8222 is only host-mapped in `docker-compose.debug.yml`, not the standard compose (which uses `expose` only).

**Fix:** Change to `GET http://localhost:8222/healthz` and add a comment: `# Requires debug compose overlay: docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.debug.yml up`

### 3. Missing environment variables

`{{requestId}}` (used in social.http for accept/decline) and `{{nextCursor}}` (used in activity.http for pagination) are referenced but not in `http-client.env.json`.

**Fix:** Add placeholder entries to the env file:
```json
"requestId": "<paste-from-friend-request-response>",
"nextCursor": "<paste-from-first-feed-response>"
```

title: Error handling and offline state management
description:
Define the shared frontend runtime behavior for errors, offline state, retries, and session recovery. This issue should keep major screens from inventing their own token-refresh and error-handling logic.

## Shared API/Session Layer
- [ ] Centralized API client with consistent request/response/error handling.
- [ ] Session bootstrap on app load using the supported auth refresh flow.
- [ ] Queue/retry behavior for requests interrupted by token refresh.
- [ ] Timeout and retry handling for transient failures.

## Error And Offline UX
- [ ] Friendly, actionable error messaging that aligns with the product tone.
- [ ] Shared loading/empty/error presentation patterns for major screens.
- [ ] Offline detection, subtle offline indicators, and graceful disabling/retry behavior.
- [ ] React error boundaries and safe fallback UI for unexpected client crashes.

## Logging And Diagnostics
- [ ] Structured client-side logging for token-refresh failures, retried requests, surfaced API errors, and offline/online transitions during E2E/debug runs.
- [ ] Document how downstream screens should hook into the shared runtime instead of bypassing it.

## Notes
- Server remains the source of truth; this is graceful recovery, not offline-first sync.
- The goal is consistency and recoverability across screens.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
Major frontend flows can rely on one shared session/error/offline runtime.
Tests cover refresh/retry behavior, offline transitions, and user-facing error presentation.
Client-side diagnostics are detailed enough to explain failed auth/network flows during debugging and E2E runs.
close_reason: Frontend error handling with ErrorBoundary and OfflineIndicator implemented

title: Consistency calculation algorithm (60-day rolling window)
description:
Define and implement the consistency calculation that powers The Flame.

## Core Algorithm
- [ ] Calculate: (actual completions / expected completions) over rolling window
- [ ] Default window: 60 days
- [ ] Result: percentage 0-100

## Edge Cases
- [ ] New habits (<60 days): Use available days as denominator
- [ ] Habits started today: Show 0% or 'Just started' state
- [ ] Weekly habits: Expected = weeks in window, not days
- [ ] Custom frequency: Calculate expected based on selected days

## The Flame Mapping
- [ ] Define percentage to Flame intensity mapping
- [ ] 'Grows quickly': Fast response to improvement
- [ ] 'Shrinks slowly': Gradual decline, forgiving

## Example Calculations
Daily habit, 60-day window:
- 60/60 completions = 100% (blazing)
- 45/60 completions = 75% (strong)
- 30/60 completions = 50% (medium)
- 6/60 completions = 10% (ember)

Weekly habit (1x per week), 60-day window (~8 weeks):
- 8/8 completions = 100%
- 6/8 completions = 75%

## Implementation
- [ ] Backend: calculateConsistency() in Habit Service (.NET)
- [ ] Returned as part of GET /habits/{id}/stats response
- [ ] Frontend: getFlameIntensity(consistencyPercentage) for visual mapping
- [ ] Consider caching with smart invalidation


## Timezone Handling (CRITICAL)
- [ ] Consistency window uses the user's LOCAL calendar dates, not UTC
- [ ] '60-day window start' = midnight of (today - 60 days) in user's timezone
- [ ] Client must supply IANA timezone (e.g. America/New_York) with every completion
- [ ] Stored as local_date column on the completions table for fast window queries
- Edge case: user logs at 11 PM UTC-5 → server sees 4 AM next day UTC → must use local_date, not completed_at

## Notes
- This replaces streaks as the PRIMARY metric
- Implemented in the Habit Service backend, consumed by frontend
- No streak counters anywhere in the app — consistency is the only metric

(Originally GH RewardStack #72)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The scope described above is complete end-to-end.
- [ ] Tests, documentation, and configuration updates required by this work are included.
- [ ] Relevant quality gates pass for the changed areas.
acceptance_criteria:
The consistency algorithm defines one clear backend/frontend contract, including the response fields needed by flame and stats consumers.
Tests cover boundary math, timezone-sensitive windows, and representative daily/weekly/custom-frequency scenarios.
The frontend-facing flame/intensity mapping inputs are explicit enough that downstream components do not reinterpret the rules differently.
close_reason: Flame intensity mapping with asymmetric thresholds and 54 new tests

title: Honest Minimums — truthful fallback completions that keep the ember alive
description:
Let a habit define an optional "Honest Minimum": a smaller, specific version of the habit that counts as showing up on hard days without pretending it is the same as a full completion. This is not a streak-saving loophole and not a fake win. It is a truthful middle state between a full completion and a total miss.

## Core Concept
- Each habit can optionally define one Honest Minimum, such as "10-minute walk" for a 45-minute workout or "read 2 pages" for "read 20 pages"
- On a given day, the user can log either a full completion or an Honest Minimum
- Honest Minimums contribute less than full completions but more than zero to the habit's consistency/flame through one explicit shared weighting model
- UI and history must always distinguish full completions from Honest Minimums
- A habit sustained only on Honest Minimums should not look equivalent to one sustained on full completions

## Product Philosophy
This feature should operationalize Winzy's core belief that consistency matters more than perfection while still preserving honesty.

It should help users avoid all-or-nothing collapse:
- full completion > honest minimum > miss
- but honest minimum must never be framed as fully completing the habit

## V1 Scope
- Add optional Honest Minimum configuration to the existing create/edit habit flows
- Update the habit completion model so a logged day can be `full`, `minimum`, or `none`
- Today screen supports logging a full completion or an Honest Minimum for the current day
- Habit Detail supports viewing and correcting minimum vs full completions within the existing editable window
- Flame, consistency, and stats surfaces use one shared minimum-weighted contract instead of treating minimums as binary full wins
- Supportive copy makes it clear that the user kept the ember alive without claiming they fully did the habit

## Behavioral Rules
- Honest Minimum configuration should be a concrete smaller action, not a vague motivational sentence
- V1 should use one documented weighting model across the product rather than per-habit custom percentages
- Users should be able to move a day between `none`, `minimum`, and `full` through the existing correction flows
- Existing flame/public/stat consumers may inherit the updated aggregate consistency, but v1 does not need a separate public-facing minimum-day narrative

## Owner Experience
- Configure Honest Minimum while creating/editing a habit or later from the habit detail/edit flow
- On Today, choose between marking the habit fully done or logging the Honest Minimum
- On Habit Detail, calendar/history clearly show whether a day was full or minimum
- Stats show both the distinction and the aggregate effect without collapsing them into one number-only story

## Architecture Notes
- Completion records need an explicit completion kind/state so backend and frontend do not guess from copy
- Habit Service and consistency math remain the source of truth; do not bolt on a frontend-only interpretation of minimums
- Flame thresholds or messaging may need recalibration once partial contributions exist, but the result must still feel forgiving and legible
- Public/share surfaces that already consume aggregate flame should only expose what the underlying visibility rules already allow

## Quality Expectations
- Backend tests cover configuration, logging, corrections, weighted consistency math, timezone boundaries, and mixed full/minimum histories
- Frontend tests cover create/edit configuration, Today interactions, Habit Detail rendering, and supportive copy
- Playwright covers configure minimum -> log minimum -> verify distinct Today/detail/stats behavior -> upgrade that day to full -> verify stronger effect, with detailed step logging
acceptance_criteria:
Users can configure and log an Honest Minimum as a distinct weaker-than-full completion state for a habit without collapsing it into a fake full win or a binary miss.
Consistency, flame, and stats surfaces reuse one explicit minimum-weighted contract so Honest Minimums matter, remain visually/semantically distinct from full completions, and never introduce streak-style regression.
Tests cover configuration, daily logging, historical correction, weighted math, supportive copy, and mixed-history edge cases well enough that product-truthfulness regressions are catchable before release.
notes:
## Non-Goals
- No arbitrary user-defined weighting percentages in v1
- No multiple minimum tiers per habit in v1
- No streak-preservation language or "X days in a row" framing
- No silently auto-converting a miss into a minimum; the user must log or correct it intentionally
- No fake marketing language that treats Honest Minimums as equivalent to full completions

## Suggested Verification
### Backend Unit / Integration
- Happy path: habit with configured Honest Minimum can log `minimum` and `full` completion kinds on different days
- Happy path: changing a day from minimum to full increases the weighted consistency/flame contribution as expected
- Happy path: removing a minimum completion returns the day to `none` cleanly
- Edge case: mixed history of full/minimum/miss days produces the documented aggregate consistency result
- Edge case: weekly/custom-frequency habits apply the minimum weighting coherently within the existing expected-completions model
- Edge case: timezone-boundary logging still assigns the correct local date for minimum completions
- Error condition: invalid completion kind transitions or malformed minimum configuration return clear validation errors
- Error condition: habits without an Honest Minimum configured cannot log `minimum` accidentally

### Frontend Unit / Component
- Happy path: create/edit habit flow lets the owner define or remove an Honest Minimum
- Happy path: Today renders distinct affordances for `full` and `minimum` logging when configured
- Happy path: Habit Detail/calendar/history render minimum days distinctly from full days
- Edge case: supportive copy for minimum days says the user showed up without claiming full completion
- Edge case: habits without a configured minimum keep the simpler full/none interaction model
- Verification: no streak language or fake-equivalence copy appears anywhere in Honest Minimum UI
- Error condition: logging or saving minimum config failures preserve context and show recoverable feedback

### E2E Tests (Playwright)
- Create or edit a habit to add an Honest Minimum
- Log an Honest Minimum from Today and verify the day is rendered distinctly in Habit Detail
- Verify stats/flame change, but less than a full completion would
- Upgrade that same day from minimum to full and verify the stronger effect
- Remove the Honest Minimum configuration and verify the extra Today affordance disappears
- Detailed step logging for configuration, logging, correction, and flame/stat assertions

title: Habit consistency truthfulness still drifts at the 60-day boundary and on day one
description:
Two core truthfulness problems remain in the habit model: the API accepts a completion date outside the visible/correctable 60-day window, and a habit created today can become effectively 100 percent or blazing after a single same-day completion.

Scope:
- Align accepted completion dates with the actual visible correction window
- Revisit the day-one consistency/flame contract so new habits do not overstate continuity
- Add tests that lock the final truthfulness rules across backend and frontend surfaces

title: Core habit flows still break archive and weekly scheduling behavior
description:
Existing core habit flows still have two major basics failures: archive from Habit Detail is a no-op in the real shell, and weekly habits created through the shipped UI do not collect/persist the schedule shape that Today filtering expects.

Scope:

title: EF Core & PostgreSQL Migrations
description:
Establish the shared EF Core + PostgreSQL migration conventions and bootstrapping pattern used by all services. This issue owns the common approach; concrete service schemas still live in the relevant service implementation beads.

## Shared EF/Migration Foundation
- [ ] Standardize package choices, DbContext registration, migration project layout, and naming conventions.
- [ ] Define shared PostgreSQL conventions: snake_case naming, UUID keys, timestamptz/date usage, and indexing expectations.
- [ ] Document the migration/bootstrapping pattern every service follows in development and CI.

## Runtime Behavior
- [ ] Services can apply pending migrations safely in the supported development workflow.
- [ ] Migration startup logs clearly show what was applied or why startup failed.
- [ ] Migration application is idempotent for a clean database and a partially initialized database.

## Validation
- [ ] At least one representative service proves the pattern end-to-end with integration coverage.
- [ ] Service-specific beads remain responsible for their own entities, schema details, and concrete migrations.
- [ ] Dev seed-data behavior is kept separate from this issue unless it is needed only to validate the migration pattern.

## Notes
- This issue should reduce duplicated EF setup work without hiding service-specific schema ownership.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
A documented migration pattern exists that service beads can reuse consistently.
Clean and partially initialized databases can boot through the supported migration path with useful logs.
The issue no longer ambiguously owns every service schema in full.
close_reason: done

Build the authentication UI screens for the web-first app.

title: Challenge Service HabitService client defaults to wrong port 5001 instead of 5002
description:
In services/challenge-service/src/Program.cs line ~37, the HabitService HttpClient defaults to http://habit-service:5001 but the Habit Service listens on port 5002. The docker-compose environment variable overrides this in practice, so it works in the stack — but host-run development or any configuration that relies on the default will fail silently.

## Fix
Change the default URL from http://habit-service:5001 to http://habit-service:5002 in the AddHttpClient registration.

## Tests
- Verify existing integration tests still pass
- Verify the service resolves habits correctly when no env override is present

## Acceptance Criteria
- The default HabitService client URL matches the actual Habit Service port (5002)
- Tests confirm habit resolution works without environment overrides
close_reason: Merged in PR #113 (Wave 4)

title: Setup testing infrastructure
description:
Configure testing infrastructure for frontend and backend. Testing is a hard gate: implementation beads should be able to rely on a real shared harness instead of inventing ad-hoc tests per feature.

## Testing Philosophy
- Every backend service gets unit tests for domain logic and validators.
- Every backend HTTP surface gets integration tests against real infrastructure.
- Messaging behavior is covered with JetStream-enabled integration tests, not only mocks.
- Frontend user flows get component/hook coverage plus Playwright journeys for critical paths.
- Failures must be diagnosable from logs and artifacts, not just a red test name.

## Frontend Testing Foundation
- [ ] Configure Jest for the Expo web-first app with shared render/test utilities.
- [ ] Provide patterns for component, hook, and screen tests.
- [ ] Standardize API, storage, and navigation mocking helpers.
- [ ] Add coverage reporting and root-level commands for local and CI runs.

## Backend Testing Foundation
- [ ] Create reusable xUnit test patterns for all services.
- [ ] Provide Testcontainers helpers for PostgreSQL and JetStream-enabled NATS.
- [ ] Add reusable helpers for HTTP assertions, DB assertions, and event assertions.
- [ ] Cover startup stream setup, publish/subscribe flows, malformed payload handling, retry/ack behavior, and dependency failure scenarios.
- [ ] Avoid the known concurrent `dotnet test` file-locking problem by serializing runs or isolating outputs when needed.

## Diagnostics And Artifacts
- [ ] Test failures emit useful logs: request/response details, affected entity IDs, emitted NATS subjects, and relevant assertion context.
- [ ] CI publishes machine-readable test results plus human-useful failure artifacts.
- [ ] Document how feature beads are expected to add or extend tests using the shared harness.

## CI Integration
- [ ] Frontend and backend test commands run from the repo root.
- [ ] Coverage/results are visible in CI checks.
- [ ] Later feature and bug beads can depend on this issue as the canonical test foundation.

## Notes
- Playwright runner/setup is tracked in winzy.ai-ftb.
- Project-scoped agent MCP config is tracked separately in winzy.ai-dy7.
- Better to tighten the harness now than rediscover infrastructure bugs during feature work.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
A shared frontend/backend test harness exists with documented local and CI entry points.
Backend integration coverage uses real PostgreSQL and JetStream-enabled NATS containers rather than mock-only messaging tests.
Failure output includes actionable logs/artifacts so feature teams can debug broken flows quickly.
close_reason: done

title: Per-habit visibility settings (frontend UI)
description:
Frontend UI for controlling who can see each habit. This issue is the client-side control surface for Social Service visibility rules.

## Visibility Options
- [ ] Private — only you can see this habit
- [ ] Friends — all approved friends can see this habit
- [ ] Public — anyone with the public flame link can see this habit

## UI Location
- [ ] Toggle in Create Habit flow for initial visibility
- [ ] Toggle in Edit Habit flow for later changes
- [ ] Clear visual indicator on habit items/details

## Implementation
- [ ] Default remains private unless the user's explicit social preference says otherwise.
- [ ] Save visibility through Social Service, which remains the source of truth.
- [ ] Read current visibility/defaults from Social Service-backed data, not an independent habit-side visibility model.

## Notes
- Privacy is the default and sharing is explicit.
- Keep the control simple: clear choices, no permission-matrix complexity.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
Users can set and update habit visibility from the create/edit flows using the Social Service source of truth.
The frontend no longer assumes a second independent visibility store.

## Unit Tests (Jest)
- Happy path: create habit flow shows visibility picker (private/friends/public)
- Happy path: default visibility comes from Social Service user preferences
- Happy path: changing visibility in edit flow calls PUT /social/visibility/{habitId}
- Happy path: visibility badge renders correct icon/text on habit items
- Edge case: Social Service down during create → habit created as private, user informed
- Edge case: visibility change fails → optimistic UI reverts to previous value with toast
- Edge case: habit created just now has no visibility row yet → defaults to private correctly
- Error condition: PUT returns 404 (habit ownership validation fails) → clear error
- Error condition: batch GET /social/visibility fails → habits shown without visibility badges

## E2E Tests (Playwright)
- Create a habit with visibility set to friends
- Verify visibility badge shows on habit list
- Edit habit visibility to public
- Check public flame page includes the now-public habit
- Set back to private and verify public flame page no longer shows it
- Detailed step logging
close_reason: Merged in PR #113 (Wave 4)

title: Local development data seeding script
description:
Create a local development seed flow that populates the running stack with realistic but deterministic data for cross-service testing and demos.

## Seed Goals
- [ ] Create a stable set of users, habits, completions, friendships, visibility settings, and at least one challenge.
- [ ] Use deterministic identities, usernames, and passwords so docs and tests can reference them consistently.
- [ ] Produce data varied enough to exercise different flame states and social scenarios.

## Execution Model
- [ ] Prefer seeding through the API Gateway so the real request path is exercised.
- [ ] Decide whether the script is idempotent, resettable, or both; document that choice clearly.
- [ ] Make the minimum required stack explicit before the seed flow runs.

## Scope Boundary
- [ ] Core deterministic coverage is required for Auth, Habit, Social, and Challenge domains.
- [ ] If derived consumers such as Notification or Activity are also seeded/verified, that must be explicit and deterministic rather than implied.
- [ ] If derived consumers are out of scope for the current seed flow, the output/docs must say so clearly to avoid misleading demos.

## Output And Diagnostics
- [ ] Print a verbose summary of created or reused entities, including stable IDs/usernames where helpful.
- [ ] Make failures easy to diagnose with step-by-step logging rather than a generic crash.
- [ ] Document how the seed data maps to common manual test and demo scenarios.

## Notes
- This is for local development and demos, not production bootstrapping.
- Keep the data realistic, but favor determinism over randomness.
acceptance_criteria:
A documented seed flow can populate deterministic core domain data across the supported local stack.
The seed output clearly states whether derived consumers such as notifications/activity were also verified or are intentionally out of scope for that run.
Manual testing and demos can rely on known seeded users/relationships, and failures are diagnosable from the script output.
close_reason: Merged in PR #113 (Wave 4)

title: Publish habit.archived NATS event for Social Service visibility cleanup
description:
ADR-001 (cross-service visibility orchestration) recommends publishing a habit.archived event when a habit is deleted/archived, so the Social Service can clean up orphaned visibility_settings rows. Currently:

- DELETE /habits/{id} in Habit Service soft-deletes (sets ArchivedAt) but publishes NO event
- Social Service has no subscriber for habit archival
- Orphaned visibility rows accumulate in social_service DB (harmless but messy)

## Scope
- Add HabitArchived subject to Contracts/Subjects.cs
- Add HabitArchivedEvent record to Contracts/HabitEvents.cs  
- Publish habit.archived from Habit Service DELETE endpoint after successful archive
- Add HabitArchivedSubscriber to Social Service that deletes the visibility_settings row
- The HABITS JetStream stream already captures habit.> so no new stream needed

## Quality Expectations
- Unit test: Habit Service publishes habit.archived on archive
- Integration test: Social Service subscriber deletes visibility row on receiving event
- Test: idempotent — re-archiving same habit doesn't fail
- Test: redelivery doesn't cause errors (DELETE WHERE is naturally idempotent)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Archiving a habit publishes a habit.archived event that triggers Social Service visibility cleanup
- Orphaned visibility rows are cleaned up within JetStream delivery window
- Tests cover the publish, subscribe, cleanup, and idempotent redelivery paths
close_reason: Merged in PR #113 (Wave 4)

comments.author: Gabko14
comments.text: Additional context from backend audit 2026-03-13: The visibility.changed and friend.removed NATS subjects are defined in Contracts/Subjects.cs and published by Social Service, but NO service subscribes to them. The VISIBILITY and FRIENDS streams will accumulate unconsumed messages. ADR-002 specifies that the Activity Service should subscribe to both for async cleanup. This bead covers habit.archived; a separate bead may be needed for wiring the existing visibility.changed and friend.removed consumers into Activity Service.

title: Observability: structured logging and request correlation
description:
Add structured HTTP/service logging and request correlation across the backend. This issue is about making request flows and background event handling diagnosable, not about redefining `/health` semantics.

## Scope
- [ ] Standardize structured request/response logging for the gateway and backend services.
- [ ] Propagate a correlation/request ID across gateway, service, and background message-handling boundaries where possible.
- [ ] Ensure HTTP logs consistently include route, method, status, latency, and relevant entity/user identifiers when safe.
- [ ] Ensure NATS/background-handler logs include subject, consumer/subscriber identity, message/correlation IDs, retry outcome, and failure reason.
- [ ] Document local and CI log expectations so failing tests and demos are easier to diagnose.

## Non-Goals
- [ ] `/health` payload truthfulness belongs to winzy.ai-2m3.
- [ ] Larger event-trace propagation changes should be tracked separately if they become necessary.

## Notes
- Good logging should make security bugs, integration failures, and flaky tests faster to understand.
- Keep personally sensitive data out of logs.
acceptance_criteria:
Gateway and backend services emit structured HTTP and background-handler logs with usable request/correlation context.
Main request and event-processing paths can be followed across gateway, service, and consumer boundaries during debugging and failing tests.
The issue stays narrowly focused on observability/correlation rather than absorbing unrelated health-check or user-copy concerns.
close_reason: Structured logging and correlation ID propagation added across all services

title: Service health wiring is inconsistent: auth requires INatsConnection that is never registered
description: auth-service adds AddNatsHealthCheck() but no service registers AddNatsMessaging(), so /health will attempt to construct NatsHealthCheck without an INatsConnection. At the same time, the other services still expose bare health checks without DB/NATS probes, so the gateway's aggregate health remains misleading. Fix service startup wiring and align all services with the documented health contract.
acceptance_criteria: Backend services either register NATS messaging before NATS health probes or intentionally omit those probes where NATS is not in scope, and /health wiring is consistent with the documented contract.\nTests cover service startup, per-service health responses, and at least one aggregated-health path with realistic dependency wiring.\nLogs make missing INatsConnection registration or broken dependency probes obvious instead of failing mysteriously.
close_reason: done

title: Web auth hardening still leaves legacy refresh tokens readable in localStorage
description:
The recent web auth fix stopped writing new refresh tokens to localStorage, but it does not actively clear old winzy_refresh_token values that may already exist from previous sessions. On web, refresh/bootstrap logic can still read that legacy token until logout or manual storage clearing, which weakens the intended XSS mitigation for upgraded users.

## Scope
- Add an explicit cleanup/migration path for legacy web refresh tokens.
- Preserve the intended session model: access token in memory, refresh token in HttpOnly cookie on web.
- Add regression coverage for users upgrading from the pre-fix storage behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Legacy web refresh tokens are cleared or ignored safely during auth/session bootstrap.
- Web refresh logic no longer depends on a stale localStorage refresh token being present.
- Tests cover the upgrade/migration path, not just fresh installs.
close_reason: Merged in PR #113 (Wave 4)

title: Auth Playwright happy-path sign-in test can pass when authentication is broken
description:
The existing Playwright test for signing in with known credentials treats either a successful authenticated landing or a visible server-error banner as a passing outcome. That turns the intended happy-path test into a false-green smoke check and can hide regressions in real login behavior, seeded-user setup, cookie/session handling, or backend availability.

## Scope
- Make the existing-credentials auth journey assert real authentication success.
- Keep detailed step logging so failures remain diagnosable in CI.
- If backend preconditions are required, make them explicit instead of silently accepting the error path.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The happy-path sign-in test fails when the user cannot actually authenticate.
- Backend/data prerequisites for the test are explicit and reproducible.
- The resulting logs still make auth failures easy to diagnose in CI.
close_reason: Fixed false-green: replaced Promise.race that accepted server-error as passing with strict assertions requiring Today screen visibility and no server-error.

title: Web auth session model still violates the intended cookie-plus-memory design
description:
Current web auth still breaks the promised security model in multiple ways: auth responses expose refresh tokens to JavaScript, access tokens are persisted in localStorage instead of memory, and logout can clear local state while the real HttpOnly-cookie session remains alive on network failure.

Scope:
- Remove refresh tokens from web-visible JSON response paths
- Restore the intended in-memory-only web access-token model
- Make logout truthfully terminate the web session or surface failure clearly
- Reconcile tracker comments/closed auth beads with the actual implemented model
close_reason: Fixed in commit 6c2d903 — cookie-plus-memory auth session model

title: Auth and gateway basics still have startup and route-policy verification gaps
description:
The auth/gateway path is materially improved but still under-proven in places that matter to trust: auth-service does not fail fast on weak or placeholder JWT secrets, and the gateway test suite still does not prove key YARP route/auth policy behavior promised by the closed gateway testing bead.

Scope:
- Add fail-fast JWT secret validation to auth-service startup
- Verify key gateway auth-exemption and protected-route mappings in tests
- Tighten cookie-path/security assertions where the session model depends on them
close_reason: Added fail-fast JWT secret validation to auth-service TokenService (rejects <32 char, empty, placeholder secrets at startup). Added RouteAuthPolicyTests to gateway verifying YARP route/auth policy behavior: public routes (register, login, refresh, habits/public, vapid-key) pass without JWT, protected routes (habits, social, challenges, notifications, activity, auth/profile) return 401 without JWT, valid JWTs pass through, expired/wrong-key/garbage tokens are rejected, route ordering is correct.

title: Setup Husky pre-commit hooks
description:
Configure Husky to run quality checks before commits and pushes in the mono-repo.

## Setup
- [ ] Install husky: npm install -D husky
- [ ] Initialize husky: npx husky init
- [ ] Add pre-commit hook
- [ ] Add pre-push hook

## Pre-commit Checks (fast, <10 seconds)
- [ ] Frontend: Run ESLint on staged files (lint-staged)
- [ ] Backend: Run dotnet format --verify-no-changes on staged .cs files

## Pre-push Checks (thorough)
- [ ] Frontend: npx tsc --noEmit + npm test
- [ ] Backend: dotnet build + dotnet test on changed service projects
- [ ] Fail push if any test fails

## Configuration
- [ ] Configure lint-staged for fast pre-commit checks
- [ ] Scope pre-push checks to changed services only (detect changed paths)
- [ ] Skip hooks with --no-verify when needed (document in CLAUDE.md)

## Notes
- Pre-commit = fast formatting/lint (catches typos before they enter history)
- Pre-push = full test suite (catches bugs before they reach remote)
- No code reaches the remote without passing tests

(Originally GH RewardStack #34)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The scope described above is complete end-to-end.
- [ ] Tests, documentation, and configuration updates required by this work are included.
- [ ] Relevant quality gates pass for the changed areas.
acceptance_criteria:
Pre-commit and pre-push hooks run the intended fast-vs-thorough checks without surprising contributors or duplicating unrelated CI logic.
Changed-path scoping, skip guidance, and local setup/documentation are clear enough that the hooks help rather than block routine work.
The hook scripts themselves are tested or validated well enough that failures are diagnosable and reproducible.

title: Concurrent br writes during multi-agent audit can corrupt the local tracker DB
description:
During the basics-hardening audit, concurrent br writes from multiple agents corrupted the local SQLite tracker DB and forced a rebuild from JSONL. The current project workflow assumes several br operations are safe per teammate, so this needs a documented or technical fix.

Scope:
- Reproduce and document the multi-agent write pattern that corrupts the DB
- Define the safe tracker-write workflow for agent teams, including recovery expectations
- Add whichever lock, serialization, or operational guard is needed so audit/review workflows do not risk tracker data integrity

comments.author: Gabko14
comments.text: Fresh-eyes follow-up: after the concurrent-write corruption and DB rebuild, a forced `br sync --flush-only --force` exported 224 issues from a DB that still contained 225 issue rows and silently dropped `winzy.ai-rt9.2` from `.beads/issues.jsonl`. I manually restored that record from backup and re-imported JSONL to get DB/JSONL back to 225/225. This bead should capture not only the concurrent-write corruption trigger, but also the unsafe/stale export behavior that can follow during recovery.

title: Activity Service Implementation
description:
Implement the Activity Service (.NET Minimal API + PostgreSQL).

## Endpoints
- [ ] GET /feed — Own activity feed (paginated, friends only)

## Activity Record Model (activity_db)
- feed_entries table: id, actor_id, event_type, data (jsonb), created_at
- Backend stores structured activity types/data; frontend surfaces own the final rendered sentences/copy.

## NATS Subscriptions
- [ ] user.registered → create a structured joined activity event when in scope
- [ ] habit.created → create a structured new-habit activity event
- [ ] habit.completed → create a structured habit-completed activity event
- [ ] friend.request.accepted → create a structured friendship activity event
- [ ] challenge.created → create a structured challenge-created activity event
- [ ] challenge.completed → create a structured challenge-completed activity event
- [ ] user.deleted → delete feed entries owned by or no longer safely visible because of that user

## Feed Logic
- [ ] Only show entries from friends (query Social Service for friend list)
- [ ] Respect habit visibility settings
- [ ] Paginate with cursor-based pagination
- [ ] Any cached/denormalized friend or visibility data must invalidate safely on friend removal and visibility changes

title: Improve deriveDeviceId hash to use Web Crypto SHA-256
description: The current deriveDeviceId in frontend/src/utils/push.ts uses a 32-bit JS hash (bitwise | 0). Birthday-problem collisions become probable around ~65k endpoints per user. Replace with crypto.subtle.digest('SHA-256', ...) and take first 12 hex chars (48 bits). Low priority -- collision requires 65k+ devices per user which is unrealistic, but trivial to fix. Flagged by R6 review.

title: Count-based challenge progress is not idempotent for redelivered habit.completed events
description:
`HabitCompletedSubscriber` increments `CompletionCount++` for `DaysInPeriod` and `TotalCompletions` without deduplicating the specific completion being processed. Under JetStream's at-least-once delivery, a redelivered `habit.completed` event can increment the same challenge twice and overstate progress or complete a challenge early.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Count-based challenge progress is idempotent when the same completion event is redelivered.
- Tests cover a redelivery/retry path and prove `CompletionCount` does not inflate.
- The deduplication key or approach is explicit enough to maintain safely.
close_reason: CompletionCount tracked via idempotent processed-dates JSON set

title: Habit Service Implementation
description:
Implement the Habit Service (.NET Minimal API + PostgreSQL).

## Endpoints
- [ ] POST /habits — Create new habit
- [ ] GET /habits — List own habits
- [ ] GET /habits/{id} — Habit details
- [ ] PUT /habits/{id} — Update habit
- [ ] DELETE /habits/{id} — Delete/archive habit
- [ ] POST /habits/{id}/complete — Mark habit complete (body: { date?: string, timezone: string } — timezone REQUIRED to resolve 'today' correctly for the user's locale)
- [ ] DELETE /habits/{id}/completions/{date} — Remove a completion (for corrections via calendar view)
- [ ] GET /habits/{id}/stats — Consistency stats (60-day rolling window)
- [ ] GET /habits/user/{userId} — Internal: habits for a user (called by Social Service)
- [ ] GET /habits/public/{username} — Public flame data for shareable profiles, backed by exact username resolution plus visibility-aware read logic

## Database Schema (habit_db)
- habits table: id, user_id, name, icon, color, frequency, custom_days, created_at, archived_at
- completions table: id, habit_id, user_id, completed_at, local_date, note

## Consistency Algorithm
- [ ] 60-day rolling window calculation
- [ ] Handle edge cases: new habits, weekly frequency, custom days
- [ ] Return percentage 0-100
- [ ] All window boundary calculations use the user's supplied IANA timezone

## Timezone Handling
- completions.completed_at stored as timestamptz (UTC) in DB
- completions.local_date stores the user's local calendar date when the completion was logged
- Backend derives 'today' and 60-day window start from the user's supplied timezone, not server UTC
- Prevents: user in UTC-5 logging at 10 PM being counted as the next day

## Cross-Service Responsibilities
- [ ] Habit Service owns habit data and consistency calculations.
- [ ] Social Service owns visibility rules and privacy defaults; Habit Service must not become the source of truth for visibility state.
- [ ] Public flame reads can use an internal composition/projection, but they must respect Auth username identity and Social visibility rules.

## NATS Events
- [ ] Publish habit.created { userId, habitId, name }
- [ ] Publish habit.completed { userId, habitId, date, consistency }
- [ ] Subscribe user.deleted → delete all habits and completions for userId

## Authorization
- [ ] user_id from X-User-Id header (set by Gateway)
- [ ] Users can only access their own habits
- [ ] GET /habits/user/{userId} is internal-only (no auth check, trusted network)

## Notes
- Core service of the application
- Consistency calculation is the heart of the product (replaces streaks)

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
Habit creation, completion, stats, and deletion flows in the issue description work end-to-end with correct timezone handling.
Tests cover consistency math, timezone boundaries, internal/public endpoint contracts, and event-handler behavior including idempotency or safe retry handling where applicable.
Structured logs make habit-command handling and event processing diagnosable by subject, entity IDs, and failure reason.
notes:
## Done When
- [ ] All 9 endpoints return correct status codes
- [ ] 60-day rolling window consistency calculation works for daily, weekly, custom
- [ ] Edge cases handled: new habits, habits started today, weekly frequency
- [ ] habit.created and habit.completed events publish via NATS
- [ ] GET /habits/user/{userId} internal endpoint works (for Social Service)
- [ ] DELETE /habits/{id}/completions/{date} removes completion and recalculates consistency
- [ ] GET /health responds with DB + NATS status
- [ ] Dockerfile builds and runs in Docker Compose
- [ ] Integration tests for every endpoint (Testcontainers)
- [ ] Unit tests for consistency algorithm (all edge cases)
- [ ] 80%+ code coverage

title: Notifications screen is a navigation dead end in the authenticated shell
description:
The notifications bell now opens NotificationScreen from RootNavigator, but the screen provides no back/home/profile navigation and RootNavigator does not render any escape hatch while `screen === "notifications"`. Users can reach the inbox but cannot return to Today without reloading the app.

## Evidence
- frontend/src/navigation/RootNavigator.tsx switches to `screen === "notifications"` and renders NotificationScreen without an `onBack` prop or any surrounding shell controls.
- frontend/src/components/notifications/NotificationScreen.tsx has no back button or navigation affordance of its own.

## Impact
This regresses the authenticated shell into a dead-end route and undermines the goal of wiring notifications into the real app navigation.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] At least one Playwright journey covers opening the inbox and navigating back out through the shipped UI.
- [ ] The navigation model remains consistent with the existing RootNavigator state machine or a deliberate replacement.

title: Shared NATS subscriber consumes entire wildcard streams instead of filtering to one subject
description:
`shared/Winzy.Common/Messaging/JetStreamSetup.cs` creates broad streams (`user.>`, `habit.>`, `friend.>`, `challenge.>`), while `shared/Winzy.Common/Messaging/NatsEventSubscriber.cs` creates consumers without configuring `FilterSubject`/`FilterSubjects`. JetStream therefore delivers every subject in that stream to the consumer. A subscriber intended for `user.deleted` on the `USERS` stream would also receive `user.registered`; a `habit.completed` subscriber on `HABITS` would also receive `habit.created`.

This is not only a routing bug. It sits in the same subscriber surface area that previously needed resilience fixes, so the final plan must ensure wrong-subject or incompatible payloads are handled safely and logged clearly rather than killing the consumer loop.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Create a subscriber derived from `NatsEventSubscriber<T>` for one event type on a wildcard stream such as `USERS`.
2. Publish a different event on another subject within the same stream (for example `user.registered`).
3. Observe the consumer still receives that message because no subject filter was configured.

## Notes
- This blocks every service that relies on subject-specific JetStream consumers.
- The fix should verify both routing correctness and failure isolation.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
`NatsEventSubscriber` requires/configures an explicit subject filter for the intended event subject(s).
Wrong-subject or incompatible payload deliveries are logged and isolated safely rather than killing the subscriber loop.
At least one real JetStream integration test proves a consumer such as `user.deleted` ignores `user.registered` while the consumer stays healthy.
close_reason: done
